artifacts.prd:
# Joke Engine — Product Requirements Document

---

## Executive Summary

Joke Engine is an AI-powered humor platform that generates, curates, and delivers contextually appropriate comedy content at scale — programmatically, on demand, and with audience-aware intelligence. Built on Google Cloud Platform and powered by Vertex AI's Gemini models, Joke Engine serves two parallel markets simultaneously: a consumer-facing entertainment product where individuals discover personalized humor, and a developer API platform where engineers can embed context-sensitive jokes into their own applications, chatbots, and digital experiences with a few lines of code.

The platform addresses a genuine gap in the developer tooling ecosystem. Humor is one of the most effective mechanisms for user engagement, brand personality, and retention — yet there is no reliable, programmable, production-grade source of contextual comedy. Existing solutions are static libraries that go stale, general-purpose LLM calls that lack safety guarantees, or manual content teams that don't scale. Joke Engine replaces all of these with a single, intelligent system that generates original humor, filters it for safety and appropriateness, tags it for precise retrieval, and learns continuously from user feedback through a reinforcement learning pipeline.

The business model is API-first: developers and product teams subscribe to access the Joke Engine REST API, with SDKs provided for JavaScript and Python at launch. Consumer access drives the training data flywheel — real ratings and engagement signals feed back into Vertex AI fine-tuning jobs that make every subsequent joke smarter, safer, and more relevant. This creates a compounding competitive moat where Joke Engine becomes measurably better the more it is used. The target delivery window is 10 weeks to a production-ready MVP, with an estimated infrastructure operating cost of $800–$2,200/month on GCP.

---

## The Problem

Developers, content creators, and businesses currently lack a reliable, programmable source of contextual, audience-appropriate humor that can be integrated into applications, chatbots, and digital experiences without manual curation.

**For developers**, integrating humor into an application today means maintaining a static joke library that becomes outdated within months, writing complex prompt chains against general-purpose LLMs with no safety guarantees, and building custom filtering logic that still can't filter by tone, topic, or audience suitability programmatically. There is no humor-native API with authentication, versioning, rate limiting, and SDK support — the table-stakes infrastructure that developers expect from any production service.

**For content creators and comedians**, the creative process is bottlenecked by writer's block, time-consuming audience research, and the absence of any feedback mechanism to measure which joke variations land with which demographics before performing them. Comedy writing is iterative by nature, but the tools available to professional and aspiring comedians are no different from what was available twenty years ago: a blank page and gut instinct.

**For product managers and app owners**, adding personality and humor to a product is frequently prioritized and just as frequently deprioritized because the execution risk is high. A single offensive or tone-deaf joke in a consumer application can trigger brand damage and user churn. Without content filtering guarantees, audience segmentation controls, and engagement analytics, humor is treated as a liability rather than an asset — even though data consistently shows it drives retention and satisfaction when executed well.

---

## The Solution

Joke Engine solves these problems through a vertically integrated humor intelligence platform with three interlocking layers.

The **generation layer** uses Vertex AI Gemini models with structured prompt engineering and fine-tuned humor-specific variants to produce original, contextually grounded jokes on demand. Unlike raw LLM calls, the generation engine accepts structured parameters — topic, tone, style, audience age group, content strictness level — and returns jokes that conform to those specifications reliably.

The **safety and classification layer** runs synchronously in the request path before any content is delivered. Every generated joke passes through GCP Natural Language API safety classifiers and a custom fine-tuned moderation model hosted on Cloud Run. This is not a post-hoc review queue — harmful content is blocked at the source, guaranteeing a less than 0.1% false negative rate as a hard service-level objective.

The **learning layer** closes the feedback loop. User ratings (thumbs up/down, star ratings) are ingested through Cloud Pub/Sub and flow into scheduled Vertex AI fine-tuning jobs that continuously improve joke quality, personalization accuracy, and safety recall. The more Joke Engine is used, the better it becomes — creating a data advantage that compounds over time and is structurally difficult for competitors to replicate.

---

## User Personas

### Alex Rivera — Developer / Software Engineer

**Goals:**
- Integrate contextually appropriate humor into a customer-facing chatbot without building custom NLP infrastructure
- Access a well-documented REST API with SDK support that works reliably at production scale
- Filter jokes programmatically by tone and audience suitability to match the app's brand voice

**Pain Points:**
- No humor-specific API exists with the reliability and documentation standards expected of a production service
- Static joke libraries go stale quickly and lack filtering capabilities beyond basic categories
- General-purpose LLM calls require custom prompt engineering, safety filtering, and response parsing — significant undifferentiated work
- Rate limiting and cost unpredictability make LLM-based humor impractical for high-volume applications

**How Forge Helps:**
Alex installs the `joke-engine` npm package in under five minutes, authenticates with an API key, and makes a single function call with parameters specifying `audience: "professional"`, `tone: "dry"`, and `topic: "software development"`. The response returns a rated, safe, original joke in under 200ms. Alex's chatbot now has personality. The SDK abstracts authentication, caching awareness, and error handling — Alex writes product code, not infrastructure code.

---

### Jordan Kim — Content Creator / Comedian

**Goals:**
- Generate novel joke premises and variations to break through writer's block
- Test different versions of a joke concept to identify which framing works best
- Adapt existing material for different audiences (corporate event vs. open mic vs. social media) efficiently

**Pain Points:**
- The blank page problem: starting a new set or content series requires significant time investment before the first usable idea emerges
- No structured way to A/B test joke variations and get quantitative feedback on what lands
- Adapting a joke for a different audience requires deep contextual intuition that is hard to systematize or accelerate

**How Forge Helps:**
Jordan uses the Joke Engine web interface to generate 20 variations on a pun structure about remote work in seconds, then rates each one, which simultaneously helps Jordan identify the best material and feeds the platform's improvement loop. The personalization engine learns Jordan's style preferences and starts surfacing generated material that matches their comedic voice. Over time, the platform becomes a genuine creative collaborator, not just a search engine.

---

### Morgan Chen — Product Manager / App Owner

**Goals:**
- Add brand personality and humor to a B2C app without hiring a dedicated content team
- Ensure that no joke delivered through the platform can embarrass the brand or offend users
- Understand which types of humor resonate with their specific user demographic using real data

**Pain Points:**
- Humor initiatives are frequently killed in review because the risk of a single inappropriate joke outweighs the perceived benefit
- No tooling exists to give product teams analytics on humor engagement the way they have analytics on every other content type
- Content teams cannot scale joke production to match the volume needed for personalized delivery across a large user base

**How Forge Helps:**
Morgan configures Joke Engine through the developer dashboard, setting content strictness to `family-friendly`, enabling COPPA compliance mode for underage user segments, and subscribing to the daily delivery webhook. The analytics dashboard shows which joke categories drive the highest engagement among Morgan's user base, allowing iterative refinement of humor strategy with the same rigor applied to any other product feature. The safety guarantee removes the blocker that killed previous humor initiatives.

---

## Feature Requirements

### MVP — Phase 1

| Feature | Description | Priority | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| AI Joke Generation Engine | LLM-powered joke generation via Vertex AI Gemini with structured parameters for topic, tone, style, and audience. Returns original jokes on demand with consistent formatting. | High | L |
| Content Safety & Moderation Layer | Synchronous safety filtering using GCP Natural Language API and a custom Cloud Run classifier. Blocks harmful content before delivery. Target: <0.1% false negative rate. | High | L |
| Joke Categorization & Tagging System | Automatic tagging of generated jokes by format (pun, one-liner, knock-knock, etc.), audience suitability (kids, adults, professional), and topic. Enables multi-dimensional filtering. | High | M |
| REST API with Developer SDK | Versioned REST API with OpenAPI specification, API key authentication, and rate limiting. JavaScript (npm) and Python (PyPI) SDKs at launch with auto-generated client code from OpenAPI spec. | High | L |
| Redis Caching Layer | Cloud Memorystore Redis cache for frequently requested joke type/category combinations. Required to meet P95 <200ms latency SLA on cached responses. TTL-based expiration per category. | High | M |
| User Feedback & Rating System | Thumbs up/down and star rating UI and API endpoint. Ratings stored in BigQuery and published to Cloud Pub/Sub for downstream RL pipeline consumption. | High | M |
| API Key Authentication & Rate Limiting | Stripe-integrated subscription tiers with per-tier rate limits enforced at API Gateway. Abuse prevention and scraping protection on generation endpoints. | High | M |
| COPPA Compliance Mode | Configurable flag for platforms serving users under 13. Restricts joke categories, enforces strict content filtering, and disables data collection for affected user segments. | High | S |
| Basic Web Consumer Interface | React/TypeScript frontend for direct consumer access. Supports joke generation, browsing, rating, and basic preference configuration. Deployed on Cloud Run. | High | M |

---

### Phase 2 — Post-MVP

| Feature | Description | Priority | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| Personalization & Preference Engine | Tracks humor preferences per user in Firestore across sessions. Surfaces increasingly personalized recommendations and suppresses already-seen jokes. | Medium | L |
| Joke Analytics Dashboard | Developer and admin-facing dashboard built with Recharts showing engagement metrics, top-performing joke categories, API usage by endpoint, and audience demographic breakdowns sourced from BigQuery. | Medium | M |
| Feedback Reinforcement Learning Pipeline | Cloud Pub/Sub consumer that batches user ratings and triggers scheduled Vertex AI fine-tuning jobs to improve generation quality over time. | Medium | L |
| Scheduled Joke Delivery | Allows integrators to configure recurring joke delivery via email (SendGrid), push notification (Firebase Cloud Messaging), or webhook (Cloud Tasks) on a user-defined schedule (e.g., daily joke of the day). | Low | M |
| Multi-Language Joke Generation | Extend generation engine to support jokes in non-English languages with culturally appropriate humor calibration. Requires additional fine-tuning data and safety classifier variants. | Low | XL |
| Joke Variation Testing (A/B Mode) | Creator-facing feature to generate multiple variations of a joke premise and compare engagement metrics across variants. Structured for content creators optimizing material. | Low | M |
| Enterprise API Tier | Dedicated rate limits, SLA guarantees, custom content policy configuration, private joke libraries, and invoice-based billing for enterprise customers. | Medium | M |

---

## Technical Requirements

### Architecture Overview

Joke Engine is built as a microservices system deployed on Google Cloud Platform. Each concern — generation, safety, caching, feedback, scheduling — is an independently deployable Cloud Run service with its own scaling profile. The API Gateway (GCP Cloud Endpoints) handles routing, authentication, and rate limiting as the single entry point for all external traffic.

```
Developer / API Consumer ──► API Gateway (Cloud Endpoints)
End User / Consumer App  ──► React Frontend ──► API Gateway
Admin / Content Manager  ──► Analytics Dashboard ──► API Gateway

API Gateway ──► Auth Service
            ──► Joke API Service ──► Redis Cache (Memorystore)
                                ──► AI Generation Engine ──► Vertex AI / Gemini
                                ──► Safety Filter ──► GCP NL API + Custom Classifier
                                ──► Tagging Service
                                ──► Personalization Engine ──► Firestore

Feedback Service ──► BigQuery
                 ──► Cloud Pub/Sub ──► Vertex AI Training (fine-tuning)

Scheduler Service ──► Cloud Tasks ──► Email / Push / Webhook
```

### Performance

- **P95 API latency for cached responses:** < 200ms (hard SLA)
- **P95 API latency for uncached generation requests:** < 2,000ms
- **Safety filter overhead budget:** 20–40ms synchronous, within the 200ms cached budget
- **Cache hit target:** > 70% for production traffic on common joke category/parameter combinations
- **Uptime SLA:** 99.9% for API endpoints

### Security

- All API access requires authentication via API key (developer tier) or JWT (consumer tier) validated at the API Gateway layer
- API keys are scoped to subscription tier with per-key rate limits stored in Redis
- COPPA compliance mode disables behavioral tracking and personalisation for flagged user segments
- All data in transit encrypted via TLS 1.3; all data at rest encrypted using GCP-managed keys (CMEK available on Enterprise tier)
- Safety classifier runs synchronously and is not bypassable by any API parameter or tier configuration
- Abuse prevention: generation endpoint rate-limited per key, with IP-level circuit breakers for anomalous request patterns

### Scalability

- All services deployed on GCP Cloud Run with auto-scaling from zero to maximum instance count based on request load
- Cloud Spanner as the joke library store provides horizontal read scalability as API volume grows toward millions of monthly requests without connection pool bottlenecks
- BigQuery analytics store scales to petabyte-range feedback data with no operational intervention
- Vertex AI Training jobs are scheduled and isolated from the serving path — model updates do not require downtime
- Terraform-managed infrastructure enables environment parity across development, staging, and production with reproducible deployments

### Technology Stack

| Layer | Technology |
|---|---|
| Frontend | React, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS, Recharts |
| API Services | Node.js, Express |
| ML Services | Python, FastAPI |
| Joke Library DB | Cloud Spanner |
| Auth DB | Cloud SQL (PostgreSQL) |
| User Profiles | Firestore |
| Analytics & Feedback | BigQuery |
| Cache | Cloud Memorystore (Redis) |
| AI Generation | Vertex AI — Gemini API |
| Model Fine-Tuning | Vertex AI Training |
| Safety Filtering | GCP Natural Language API + Custom Cloud Run Classifier |
| Async Messaging | Cloud Pub/Sub |
| Scheduled Delivery | Cloud Tasks |
| Email Delivery | SendGrid |
| Push Notifications | Firebase Cloud Messaging |
| API Subscriptions | Stripe |
| Infrastructure | GCP Cloud Run, Cloud Endpoints, Cloud Build, Docker, Terraform |

### Key Architectural Decisions

**Microservices over monolith:** The joke engine has fundamentally different scaling profiles per concern. AI generation is GPU/LLM-bound; caching is I/O-bound; analytics are batch-oriented. A monolith would force uniform scaling across all of these, wasting compute and creating deployment coupling. Independent Cloud Run services scale each concern appropriately.

**Synchronous safety filter over async moderation queue:** The <0.1% false negative requirement and zero-delivery guarantee for harmful content cannot be met with an async approach where content is delivered first and reviewed second. The 20–40ms safety filter latency is budgeted within the overall 200ms cached response target.

**REST over GraphQL:** Primary consumers are third-party developers integrating via SDK. REST is universally understood, simpler to rate-limit at the gateway, and maps cleanly to OpenAPI-based SDK auto-generation. GraphQL's flexibility would introduce over-fetching vectors that complicate abuse prevention.

**Cloud Spanner over PostgreSQL for joke library:** At scale, PostgreSQL on Cloud SQL hits connection and throughput ceilings for a high-read API serving workload. Spanner provides strong consistency for deduplication and relational tag filtering that Firestore cannot support, with horizontal scalability PostgreSQL cannot match.

**Async feedback RL pipeline over real-time reinforcement learning:** Real-time RL on every rating event is operationally complex and cost-prohibitive at launch-phase volumes. Pub/Sub-buffered batch feedback into scheduled Vertex AI fine-tuning runs achieves the learning loop goal with acceptable latency (hours to days for model updates) at a fraction of the cost.

---

## Success Metrics & KPIs

| Metric | Target | Measurement Method |
|---|---|---|
| API request volume | 100,000 calls/month within 6 months of launch | Cloud Endpoints API usage logs → BigQuery |
| Average joke rating | > 3.8 / 5.0 stars across all categories | Aggregated rating scores in BigQuery, surfaced in analytics dashboard |
| Content safety false negative rate | < 0.1% | Human review sample audits + adversarial test suite run weekly |
| Third-party SDK adoption | ≥ 50 integrated applications within 12 months | Unique API key registrations with >100 requests/month |
| Consumer product week-over-week retention | ≥ 40% | Cohort retention analysis

key_decisions.title: GCP-Native Infrastructure over AWS or Azure
key_decisions.choice: Google Cloud Platform with Cloud Run, Vertex AI, and managed GCP services
key_decisions.rationale: User explicitly requested GCP. Beyond that constraint, GCP offers native Vertex AI with Gemini access, which reduces LLM integration latency and cost versus routing through a third-party API from AWS. Cloud Run's serverless container model also fits the variable traffic profile of an API product without paying for idle compute.

key_decisions.title: Microservices over Monolith
key_decisions.choice: Microservices with independently deployable Cloud Run services
key_decisions.rationale: The joke engine has distinct scaling profiles per concern: AI generation is GPU/LLM-bound, caching is I/O-bound, and analytics are batch. A monolith would force all of these to scale together wastefully. Microservices allow the generation engine to scale independently from the feedback pipeline or the scheduler service, and allows the safety filter to be updated or swapped without a full redeploy.

key_decisions.title: Cloud Spanner for Joke Library over PostgreSQL or Firestore
key_decisions.choice: Cloud Spanner as the primary joke content store
key_decisions.rationale: The joke library needs strong consistency for deduplication and tagging lookups, high read throughput for API serving, and global scalability as API volume grows toward millions of requests. PostgreSQL on Cloud SQL would hit connection and throughput limits at scale. Firestore lacks strong relational query support needed for multi-dimensional tag filtering. Spanner provides the best of both without sacrificing GCP-native tooling.

key_decisions.title: Redis (Memorystore) Caching Layer for Sub-200ms Latency SLA
key_decisions.choice: Cloud Memorystore Redis as the primary joke cache with TTL-based expiration
key_decisions.rationale: The technical constraint requiring P95 under 200ms for cached responses cannot be reliably met by hitting the LLM or even the database on every request. Redis in-process caching of frequently requested joke type/category combinations is the standard pattern here. Alternatives like CDN edge caching were considered but rejected because jokes involve auth context and personalization that vary per user, making a shared CDN cache ineffective.

key_decisions.title: Synchronous Safety Filter in Request Path over Async Post-Processing
key_decisions.choice: Content safety check runs synchronously before any joke is returned to the caller
key_decisions.rationale: The requirements explicitly mandate zero delivery of harmful content and a less than 0.1% false negative rate. An async moderation approach (flag and remove later) would allow unsafe content to be delivered in the window before review. GCP Natural Language API and a fine-tuned safety classifier on Cloud Run add ~20-40ms of latency but are acceptable given the 200ms budget on cached requests and the safety-critical nature of the constraint.

key_decisions.title: BigQuery for Feedback and Analytics over PostgreSQL or a SaaS Tool
key_decisions.choice: BigQuery as the analytics and feedback data warehouse
key_decisions.rationale: Feedback data feeds a reinforcement learning pipeline and also drives the developer analytics dashboard. This requires both high write throughput for incoming ratings and fast analytical queries over large datasets. BigQuery's serverless columnar model handles both without operational overhead. Alternatives like Mixpanel or Amplitude were rejected because the feedback data needs to be queryable by the ML pipeline, not just dashboarded, and exporting to a third party creates latency and data residency concerns.

key_decisions.title: REST API over GraphQL
key_decisions.choice: REST with versioned endpoints and OpenAPI specification
key_decisions.rationale: The primary consumers are third-party developers integrating via SDK. REST is universally understood, easier to document, and simpler to rate-limit and cache at the gateway level. GraphQL would add query complexity and potential over-fetching vectors that complicate rate limiting and abuse prevention. REST also aligns with the SDK generation workflow since OpenAPI specs auto-generate client code for JavaScript, Python, and other target languages.

key_decisions.title: Pub/Sub + Vertex AI Training for Feedback Loop over Real-Time RL
key_decisions.choice: Asynchronous feedback ingestion via Cloud Pub/Sub feeding scheduled Vertex AI fine-tuning jobs
key_decisions.rationale: Real-time reinforcement learning on every thumbs up/down is technically complex, expensive, and overkill for the launch-phase volume. Batching feedback through Pub/Sub into periodic fine-tuning runs on Vertex AI gives the learning loop benefit at a fraction of the cost and operational complexity. The feedback latency (hours to days for model updates) is acceptable for a humor preference system where gradual improvement is the goal.

# Work Orders

**Total Story Points:** 144 | **Sprints:** 5 | **Stories:** 16

---

## Epic 1: Core Infrastructure & API Foundation
> Establish the GCP-native microservices backbone, API gateway, authentication, and developer-facing REST API contract that all other epics depend on.

### [E1-S1] GCP Infrastructure Provisioning with Terraform
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** infrastructure, backend, devops

**User Story**
As a platform engineer, I want GCP infrastructure defined as code so that environments are reproducible, auditable, and deployable without manual console work.

**Work Description**
Write Terraform modules to provision all required GCP resources: Cloud Run services (joke-api, auth-svc, safety-filter, tagging-svc, personalization-svc, scheduler-svc), Cloud Memorystore Redis instance, Cloud Spanner instance and joke-library database, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance for auth, Firestore database, BigQuery datasets (analytics, feedback), Cloud Pub/Sub topics and subscriptions (feedback-events), and Cloud Tasks queues. Configure GCP Cloud Build CI/CD pipeline with triggers on main and staging branches. All resources should be tagged with environment, service, and cost-center labels to support cost tracking against the $800–2,200/month budget.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] `terraform apply` from a clean project provisions all services with zero manual steps
- [ ] Cloud Run services for joke-api, auth-svc, and safety-filter deploy successfully and pass health checks
- [ ] Cloud Memorystore Redis, Cloud Spanner, Cloud SQL, Firestore, and BigQuery resources are created with appropriate IAM bindings scoped per service
- [ ] Cloud Build pipeline triggers on push to `main` and `staging`, builds Docker images, and deploys to respective Cloud Run services
- [ ] Terraform state is stored remotely in GCS bucket with state locking enabled

---

### [E1-S2] Auth Service with JWT and API Key Management
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, security

**User Story**
As a developer integrating the Joke Engine API, I want to authenticate with an API key so that my requests are authorized and my usage is tracked against my account.

**Work Description**
Build the Auth Service as a Node.js/Express Cloud Run service backed by Cloud SQL PostgreSQL. Implement two auth flows: (1) consumer user auth via JWT (registration, login, refresh token rotation stored in PostgreSQL), and (2) developer API key issuance and validation (SHA-256 hashed keys stored in PostgreSQL with associated plan tier, rate limit tier, and owner account). Expose `/auth/register`, `/auth/login`, `/auth/refresh`, `/keys/generate`, and `/keys/revoke` endpoints. The API Gateway (Cloud Endpoints) should forward an `X-API-Key` header to this service for validation on every inbound developer request.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] `POST /auth/register` and `POST /auth/login` return a signed JWT with 15-minute expiry and a refresh token with 30-day expiry
- [ ] `POST /keys/generate` returns a unique API key; only the SHA-256 hash is persisted in PostgreSQL, never the plaintext
- [ ] API key validation completes in under 10ms via an in-memory LRU cache of recently validated keys (max 10,000 entries, 5-minute TTL)
- [ ] `POST /keys/revoke` immediately invalidates the key and purges it from the LRU cache
- [ ] Auth service passes OWASP Top 10 checks for injection, broken authentication, and sensitive data exposure in a manual review

---

### [E1-S3] API Gateway Configuration and Rate Limiting
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 5
**Tags:** backend, infrastructure, security

**User Story**
As a platform operator, I want a centralized API gateway with rate limiting enforced at the edge so that the generation endpoint cannot be scraped or abused and per-plan quotas are respected.

**Work Description**
Configure GCP Cloud Endpoints as the API gateway fronting all public routes. Define an OpenAPI 2.0 spec that routes `/v1/jokes/*`, `/v1/feedback/*`, `/v1/schedule/*`, and `/auth/*` to their respective Cloud Run backends. Implement rate limiting using Cloud Memorystore Redis with a sliding window counter keyed on API key. Define three tiers: Free (100 req/day, 10 req/min), Pro (10,000 req/day, 100 req/min), Enterprise (unlimited with burst cap of 1,000 req/min). Return `429 Too Many Requests` with `Retry-After` header and remaining quota in `X-RateLimit-*` response headers.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] OpenAPI spec is valid and all defined routes resolve to correct Cloud Run backend URLs
- [ ] Free tier API key receives `429` after the 101st request within a 24-hour window with correct `Retry-After` header
- [ ] Rate limit counters are stored in Redis with sliding window precision; counters reset accurately at window boundary
- [ ] `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, and `X-RateLimit-Reset` headers are present on every API response
- [ ] Abuse simulation test (1,000 rapid requests from single key) does not allow more than the configured burst cap through

---

### [E1-S4] JavaScript and Python SDK Generation and Publishing
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, frontend, developer-experience

**User Story**
As a developer integrating the Joke Engine into my application, I want official SDKs for JavaScript and Python so that I can call the API without manually constructing HTTP requests or handling auth headers.

**Work Description**
Generate SDK client libraries from the OpenAPI spec using OpenAPI Generator. For JavaScript/TypeScript: publish as `@jokeengine/sdk` to npm with TypeScript type definitions, supporting both CommonJS and ESM. For Python: publish as `jokeengine` to PyPI with type hints and async support via `httpx`. Both SDKs must handle API key injection, automatic retry with exponential backoff on 429/503, and response deserialization into typed model objects (Joke, Rating, ScheduleConfig). Include a README with quickstart examples for each. Automate SDK regeneration and publishing as a Cloud Build step on OpenAPI spec change.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] `npm install @jokeengine/sdk` installs successfully and TypeScript types resolve without errors in a test consumer project
- [ ] `pip install jokeengine` installs successfully; `JokeEngineClient(api_key="...").jokes.get(topic="cats")` returns a typed `Joke` object
- [ ] Both SDKs automatically retry on `429` with exponential backoff, up to 3 retries, before surfacing the error to the caller
- [ ] SDK version is automatically bumped and published to npm/PyPI when the OpenAPI spec changes via Cloud Build trigger
- [ ] SDK README includes working quickstart code for at minimum: fetch a joke, filter by category, and submit a rating

---

## Epic 2: AI Joke Generation, Safety, and Tagging
> Build the AI-powered generation pipeline with synchronous safety filtering and automatic categorization that produces safe, tagged, and cacheable joke content.

### [E2-S1] AI Joke Generation Engine with Vertex AI Gemini
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** backend, ai-ml

**User Story**
As a developer calling the API, I want to request a joke by specifying topic, tone, style, and audience parameters so that I receive a contextually appropriate, original joke generated by AI.

**Work Description**
Build the Joke API Service as a Node.js/Express Cloud Run service. Implement `POST /v1/jokes/generate` accepting a JSON body with fields: `topic` (string), `tone` (enum: witty, dry, absurd, wholesome, dark), `style` (enum: one-liner, pun, knock-knock, story, roast), `audience` (enum: kids, general, adult, professional), and optional `context` (string, max 500 chars). Construct a structured prompt template that injects these parameters into a system prompt for the Vertex AI Gemini API (`gemini-1.5-flash` for cost efficiency). Implement prompt caching: cache the system prompt prefix in Vertex AI's context caching feature. Store all generated jokes in Cloud Spanner with a `cache_key` hash of the parameter combination and a `generated_at` timestamp.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] `POST /v1/jokes/generate` with valid parameters returns a joke object with fields: `id`, `text`, `setup` (nullable), `punchline` (nullable), `topic`, `tone`, `style`, `audience`, `tags[]`, `safe`, `created_at`
- [ ] Prompt template produces jokes that match the requested `style` in at least 90% of manual test cases across 50 varied inputs
- [ ] Generated jokes are persisted to Cloud Spanner before being returned to the caller
- [ ] Vertex AI API errors (rate limit, timeout) are caught and return a `503` with a human-readable error message; the caller is never exposed to raw LLM error payloads
- [ ] Generation endpoint average latency is under 1,500ms for non-cached requests measured over 20 consecutive test calls

---

### [E2-S2] Synchronous Content Safety & Moderation Filter
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, ai-ml, security

**User Story**
As a platform operator, I want every joke to pass through a content safety filter before delivery so that no offensive, discriminatory, or harmful content ever reaches an end user.

**Work Description**
Build the Safety Filter as a Python FastAPI Cloud Run service. Implement a two-stage filter: Stage 1 uses the GCP Natural Language API's `moderateText` method to score content across harm categories (toxic, insult, profanity, deathHarm, etc.) with a confidence threshold of 0.6. Stage 2 runs a fine-tuned safety classifier (distilbert-based model deployed on Cloud Run) trained on a labeled dataset of 10,000 joke pass/fail examples. A joke must pass both stages to be delivered. The Joke API Service calls this filter synchronously at `POST /internal/safety/check` before returning any joke to the caller. Flag and quarantine jokes that fail into a `quarantine` status in Cloud Spanner for human admin review.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] No joke is returned to any API caller without first receiving a `pass` status from both safety filter stages
- [ ] Safety filter adds no more than 50ms of latency at P95, measured via Cloud Monitoring custom metric
- [ ] Filter correctly blocks 100% of a test set of 50 pre-labeled offensive joke examples (zero false negatives on test set)
- [ ] Failed jokes are written to Cloud Spanner with `status=quarantine` and include the specific harm category and confidence score that triggered the block
- [ ] Safety filter service is independently deployable and scalable without redeploying the Joke API Service

---

### [E2-S3] Joke Categorization and Tagging Service
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 5
**Tags:** backend, ai-ml

**User Story**
As a developer querying the API, I want jokes to be automatically tagged with categories, audience suitability, and topics so that I can filter and retrieve jokes programmatically without manual curation.

**Work Description**
Build the Tagging Service as a Python FastAPI Cloud Run service. After a joke passes the safety filter, the Joke API Service calls `POST /internal/tags/classify` with the joke text. Implement a multi-label classifier using Vertex AI's text embedding model (`text-embedding-004`) to assign: (1) style tags from a fixed taxonomy (pun, one-liner, knock-knock, dark-humor, wordplay, observational, self-deprecating, roast, story), (2) audience tags (kids-safe, general, adult-only, professional-safe), and (3) topic tags extracted via named entity recognition (animals, tech, food, relationships, sports, etc.). Store tags as an array in the joke's Cloud Spanner record and index them for filtered queries.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Every joke stored in Cloud Spanner has at minimum one style tag, one audience tag, and one topic tag assigned
- [ ] `GET /v1/jokes?tags=pun,kids-safe&topic=animals` returns only jokes matching all specified filter criteria from Cloud Spanner
- [ ] Tagging service classifies a joke in under 200ms at P95, measured over 100 consecutive test calls
- [ ] Audience tag `kids-safe` is never assigned to any joke that contains content scored above 0.2 on any GCP Natural Language harm category
- [ ] Tag taxonomy is defined in a configuration file (not hardcoded) so new tags can be added without a code deployment

---

### [E2-S4] Redis Caching Layer for Sub-200ms Response SLA
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 3 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, infrastructure, performance

**User Story**
As a developer integrating the Joke Engine into a real-time app, I want cached joke requests to respond in under 200ms so that the API is fast enough for synchronous in-app use.

**Work Description**
Implement a two-level caching strategy in the Joke API Service. Level 1: Before calling Vertex AI for generation, compute a `cache_key` as `SHA-256(topic + tone + style + audience)` and check Cloud Memorystore Redis. If a cache hit exists, return the cached joke immediately without calling Gemini or the safety/tagging services. Set TTL to 1 hour for generated jokes and 24 hours for pre-seeded popular joke types. Level 2: For `GET /v1/jokes/random` and `GET /v1/jokes/daily`, pre-warm the cache on a 15-minute Cloud Scheduler cron by generating and caching the top 50 most-requested parameter combinations from the prior day's BigQuery analytics. Implement cache invalidation when a joke is quarantined post-delivery.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] P95 response latency for cache-hit requests is under 200ms measured via Cloud Monitoring over a 1-hour sustained load test at 50 RPS
- [ ] Cache hit rate exceeds 60% after 24 hours of simulated traffic using the top 50 pre-warmed parameter combinations
- [ ] A quarantined joke is purged from Redis within 30 seconds of its status being set to `quarantine` in Cloud Spanner
- [ ] Cache key collision rate is zero across a test set of 1,000 distinct parameter combinations (verified by checking that each key maps to the correct joke)
- [ ] Redis memory usage stays under 80% of the provisioned Memorystore instance size under peak load simulation

---

## Epic 3: User Experience, Feedback, and Personalization
> Deliver the consumer-facing React web app, joke rating system, reinforcement learning feedback loop, and personalization engine that make the product self-improving.

### [E3-S1] React Web Frontend — Joke Discovery and Browsing UI
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 3 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** frontend

**User Story**
As an end user visiting the Joke Engine web app, I want to browse, filter, and request jokes by topic and style so that I can find humor content relevant to my mood and interests.

**Work Description**
Build the React/TypeScript/Vite frontend deployed to Cloud Run (served as a static build via nginx container). Implement the following views: (1) Home — a joke card feed showing the daily joke plus 10 recent jokes, with infinite scroll; (2) Generate — a form UI with dropdowns for topic, tone, style, and audience that calls `POST /v1/jokes/generate` and animates the result into view; (3) Browse — a filterable grid of jokes from the library using tag chips as filter controls calling `GET /v1/jokes` with query params. Style with Tailwind CSS. Use React Query for data fetching with optimistic updates. Implement skeleton loading states for all async operations.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Home page loads and displays the daily joke plus feed within 2 seconds on a simulated 4G connection (Lighthouse performance score ≥ 85)
- [ ] Generate form submits valid parameters to the API, displays a loading state, and renders the returned joke card with all metadata (tags, tone, style) visible
- [ ] Browse view correctly re-fetches and re-renders results when tag filters are added or removed without a full page reload
- [ ] All interactive elements (buttons, dropdowns, filter chips) are keyboard navigable and meet WCAG 2.1 AA contrast requirements

# CryptoForge Trading Engine — Product Requirements Document

---

> **Document Version:** 1.0
> **Status:** Ready for Stakeholder Review
> **Prepared by:** Press — Forge Platform Documentation Agent
> **Estimated Delivery:** 20 Weeks
> **Complexity:** High

---

## Executive Summary

The cryptocurrency trading landscape has matured dramatically over the past decade, yet the tooling available to traders — from retail enthusiasts to institutional portfolio managers — remains fundamentally fragmented. Traders today juggle multiple exchange dashboards, third-party bots, spreadsheet-based risk tracking, and disconnected analytics tools, stitching together a workflow that is slow, error-prone, and dangerously exposed to execution failures. CryptoForge Trading Engine is built to eliminate that fragmentation entirely.

CryptoForge is a high-performance, event-driven trading platform that unifies multi-exchange connectivity, automated strategy execution, real-time risk management, and institutional-grade analytics under a single, cohesive product. The engine is architected for speed — with a Rust-powered order router and risk engine targeting sub-100ms execution latency — and for scale, capable of ingesting thousands of concurrent WebSocket market data streams without degradation. Whether a user is deploying a simple DCA bot or managing a complex multi-strategy institutional portfolio across ten exchanges simultaneously, CryptoForge provides the infrastructure to do it reliably.

The platform is designed to serve three distinct but overlapping user archetypes: retail traders seeking automation and control, quantitative traders demanding programmable strategy infrastructure, and institutional portfolio managers requiring compliance-grade auditability and analytics. This breadth of scope is matched by a modular microservices architecture that allows each domain — execution, risk, data, analytics, notifications — to scale and evolve independently. CryptoForge is not another trading dashboard. It is the operating system for modern crypto trading.

---

## The Problem

Crypto traders across all experience levels face a common, compounding problem: **the absence of a unified, high-performance trading engine that integrates execution, strategy automation, and risk management in one platform.** The consequences of this gap are both operational and financial.

Retail traders are forced to log into multiple exchange interfaces to monitor positions, execute trades manually during volatile windows, and rely on ad-hoc price alert apps with no connection to their live portfolio state. The result is emotionally driven, slow, and inconsistent trading — with no way to validate a strategy before committing real capital.

Quantitative and algorithmic traders face a different but equally severe problem: deploying automated strategies at scale requires integrating with multiple exchange APIs that each behave differently, have inconsistent rate limits, and return incompatible data formats. There is no standardized layer for risk controls, meaning a runaway bot can blow through an account before a human can intervene. Historical data for strategy validation is siloed, incomplete, or prohibitively expensive.

Institutional users face the most acute version of the problem. Without consolidated P&L dashboards, audit trails, or smart order routing, institutions operating in crypto markets suffer from excessive slippage, compliance blind spots, and manual reconciliation overhead that would be unacceptable in any other asset class. The absence of professional-grade tooling is not merely an inconvenience — it is a direct drag on alpha generation and a latent regulatory risk.

---

## The Solution

CryptoForge solves the fragmentation problem through a purpose-built, event-driven platform that treats trade execution, risk management, strategy automation, and analytics as first-class, deeply integrated concerns rather than bolted-on features.

At the infrastructure layer, a unified Exchange Adapters Layer built on top of CCXT normalizes connectivity to 10+ major exchanges — including Binance, Coinbase, Kraken, and OKX — behind a single interface. REST and WebSocket connections are managed centrally, handling rate limits, reconnection logic, and API normalization transparently. Traders interact with one coherent system regardless of which exchange their capital sits on.

The execution core is differentiated by its technology choices. The Order Router and Risk Engine are written in Rust with an async Tokio runtime, delivering deterministic sub-100ms latency without garbage collection pauses. A Kafka-based event bus decouples services while guaranteeing ordered, durable, replayable event streams — the foundation for both real-time risk calculations and historical trade auditing. Exchange API keys are protected via HashiCorp Vault with hardware-backed encryption, automatic rotation, and full access audit trails, ensuring that the most sensitive assets in the system are treated with institutional-grade security.

For strategy execution, a Python-based Strategy Engine provides a flexible scripting interface and pre-built templates, paired with a TimescaleDB-backed backtesting environment that lets users validate performance against real historical market data before deploying capital. Paper Trading mode extends this further, allowing live market simulation without financial risk. The result is a platform where the distance between strategy idea and confident live deployment is measured in hours, not weeks.

---

## User Personas

### Maya Chen — Retail Crypto Trader

**Background:** Maya is a full-time software engineer who actively trades cryptocurrency as a side income. She manages accounts across three exchanges and spends evenings manually monitoring price movements and executing trades. She has tried several independent bots but found them unreliable and difficult to configure.

**Goals:**
- Automate recurring trading strategies (DCA, grid) without writing complex code
- Monitor all exchange accounts from a single dashboard
- Receive instant alerts when price targets or stop-loss thresholds are hit
- Test a strategy on historical data before risking real money

**Pain Points:**
- Manual trading is slow and subject to emotional decision-making during volatile markets
- No unified dashboard means constant tab-switching between exchange interfaces
- No backtesting capability makes it impossible to validate a strategy's historical performance
- Price alert tools are disconnected from portfolio state and execution

**How CryptoForge Helps:**
CryptoForge's pre-built strategy templates and drag-and-configure bot framework allow Maya to deploy DCA and grid strategies across her exchange accounts in minutes. The unified portfolio dashboard consolidates her positions, P&L, and account balances in real time. Customizable alerts — delivered via Telegram or SMS — notify her the moment a threshold is crossed, with Paper Trading mode giving her confidence before she commits capital.

---

### Alex Romero — Algorithmic / Quantitative Trader

**Background:** Alex is a former fintech software engineer who now trades full-time using custom algorithmic strategies. He has built his own exchange integrations in the past but spends a disproportionate amount of time maintaining API adapters and debugging edge cases rather than developing new strategies. His biggest concern is uncontrolled losses from runaway bots.

**Goals:**
- Deploy and monitor multiple custom trading bots simultaneously from one control plane
- Access deep historical tick data for rigorous strategy backtesting
- Enforce hard risk limits (max drawdown, daily loss caps) across all active strategies
- Minimize time spent on infrastructure and exchange integration maintenance

**Pain Points:**
- Every exchange has a different API contract, creating constant integration overhead
- No centralized risk layer means a malfunctioning bot can cause uncapped losses
- Limited access to clean, normalized historical market data for backtesting
- Scaling bots across exchanges requires managing a fragile patchwork of custom code

**How CryptoForge Helps:**
The CCXT-backed Exchange Adapters Layer eliminates the per-exchange integration burden, giving Alex a normalized API surface for all connected exchanges. The scripting interface in the Strategy Engine accepts custom logic, allowing him to deploy proprietary algorithms without restriction. The Risk Engine enforces configurable hard limits — including a kill-switch that halts all trading instantly — and runs synchronously with order execution to prevent any limit breach. TimescaleDB provides years of clean OHLCV and tick data for backtesting, with metrics including Sharpe ratio, max drawdown, win rate, and ROI.

---

### Nathaniel Ward — Institutional Portfolio Manager

**Background:** Nathaniel manages a crypto fund with assets across multiple exchanges and custodians. His firm operates under increasing regulatory scrutiny and needs audit-ready trade records, consolidated performance reporting, and reliable execution at scale. Current tooling is a combination of exchange-native interfaces and manual spreadsheet reconciliation — a process that is slow, error-prone, and not fit for institutional standards.

**Goals:**
- Consolidated real-time P&L and performance analytics across all exchange accounts
- Exportable audit trails and compliance-ready trade reports
- Smart order routing to minimize slippage on large orders
- Multi-user access control with role-based permissions and full activity logging

**Pain Points:**
- Lack of audit trails creates compliance exposure and makes regulatory reporting laborious
- No tools for intelligent portfolio rebalancing across multiple assets and exchanges
- Large orders suffer high slippage due to naive single-exchange execution
- No consolidated analytics mean performance measurement requires manual data aggregation

**How CryptoForge Helps:**
CryptoForge's Portfolio & P&L Analytics Dashboard delivers real-time and historical performance data — asset allocation, realized and unrealized P&L, fee analysis — with exportable reports designed for compliance and tax purposes. The Smart Order Router distributes large orders across exchanges to minimize market impact and slippage. Role-based access control with full audit logging of all user actions and trades satisfies institutional governance requirements. KYC/AML hooks at the institutional tier support regulatory compliance across jurisdictions.

## Technical Requirements

### Performance
- **Order Execution Latency:** End-to-end order submission latency must remain consistently below **100ms** under normal operating conditions. The Rust-based Order Router and Risk Engine on AWS EC2 C6i compute-optimized instances are the primary mechanisms for achieving this SLA.
- **Market Data Throughput:** The Go-based Market Data Pipeline must sustain ingestion and normalization of data from **thousands of concurrent WebSocket streams** without throughput degradation or message loss.
- **Backtesting Performance:** Backtesting simulations against multi-year datasets must complete within a time window that supports iterative strategy development — target under 60 seconds for a standard 1-year OHLCV backtest.
- **Frontend Responsiveness:** Real-time chart updates and order book renders must reflect market data within **200ms** of ingestion, leveraging WebSocket streaming from the Market Data Hub to the React frontend.

### Security
- **API Key Encryption:** All exchange API keys must be stored in **HashiCorp Vault** with hardware-backed encryption, fine-grained access policies, secret leasing, and full read/write audit trails. Application-layer encryption alone is explicitly rejected.
- **Authentication:** All user sessions must enforce **TOTP-based 2FA**. JWT tokens must have short expiry windows with refresh token rotation.
- **Network Security:** IP whitelisting must be enforceable at the user and API key level. All internal service communication uses **gRPC over mTLS**.
- **Audit Logging:** Every user action, trade event, API key access, and configuration change must be written to an immutable audit log stored in PostgreSQL with tamper detection.
- **Vulnerability Management:** All container images must pass automated security scanning in CI/CD pipeline. Dependency audits run on every build.
- **Incident Target:** Zero critical security incidents related to API key exposure or unauthorized trade execution.

### Scalability
- **Availability SLA:** **99.9% uptime** measured monthly. Architecture must support zero-downtime deployments via Kubernetes rolling updates on AWS EKS.
- **Horizontal Scaling:** All stateless services (API Gateway, Strategy Engine, Alert Service, Backtesting Service) must be horizontally scalable via Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler with no manual intervention required.
- **Data Storage:** TimescaleDB provides automatic time-based partitioning for OHLCV and tick data. Continuous aggregates handle rollup queries. Storage capacity must be planned for **multi-year historical data** across all connected exchanges.
- **Event Bus Durability:** Kafka topics must be configured with replication factor ≥ 3 and sufficient log retention to support event replay for debugging, audit, and backtest data replay scenarios.
- **Rate Limit Compliance:** The Exchange Adapters Layer must track per-exchange API rate limit state in Redis and enforce backpressure proactively to prevent exchange bans.
- **Graceful Degradation:** The system must handle partial exchange connectivity failures without halting operation on unaffected exchanges. Order rejections, network timeouts, and partial fills must be processed via retry logic with exponential backoff and dead-letter queuing.

### Compliance & Regulatory
- KYC/AML webhook integration points must be available for the institutional access tier to support jurisdiction-specific compliance workflows.
- Trade records must be exportable in structured formats (CSV, PDF) suitable for tax reporting and regulatory submission.
- Data residency requirements must be configurable at the deployment level for institutional customers operating under specific jurisdictional constraints.

## Epic 1: Multi-Exchange Connectivity & Market Data Pipeline
> Establish the foundational exchange adapter layer and real-time market data ingestion pipeline that all other platform services depend on.

### [E1-S1] Exchange Adapter Layer with CCXT Integration
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** backend, infrastructure, go, rust

**User Story**
As an algorithmic trader, I want the platform to connect to multiple exchanges through a unified interface so that I can manage orders and receive market data without writing bespoke integration code per exchange.

**Work Description**
Build the Exchange Adapters Layer in Go using CCXT as the normalization base. Each adapter must implement a standardized interface covering REST (order placement, account balances, trade history) and WebSocket (order book, ticker, trade stream) connections. Implement per-exchange rate-limit enforcement using Redis token-bucket counters to prevent API bans. Initial target exchanges: Binance, Coinbase Advanced, Kraken, OKX.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] All four exchanges (Binance, Coinbase, Kraken, OKX) have working adapters implementing a `ExchangeAdapter` Go interface with `PlaceOrder`, `CancelOrder`, `GetBalance`, `SubscribeOrderBook`, and `SubscribeTrades` methods
- [ ] WebSocket connections auto-reconnect within 2 seconds on disconnect and emit a `ConnectionRestored` Kafka event
- [ ] Rate-limit state is stored in Redis with per-exchange, per-endpoint bucket configuration; requests exceeding limits are queued rather than dropped
- [ ] Unit tests cover happy path, partial fill, order rejection, and network timeout scenarios with mocked exchange responses
- [ ] Integration tests run against Binance and Coinbase sandbox environments and pass all order lifecycle scenarios

---

### [E1-S2] Real-Time Market Data Hub & Kafka Pipeline
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** backend, infrastructure, go, kafka

**User Story**
As a retail trader, I want live price feeds and order book updates streamed to my dashboard with minimal delay so that I can make informed trading decisions in real time.

**Work Description**
Build the Market Data Hub service in Go that subscribes to all exchange adapter WebSocket streams, normalizes data into a canonical schema (OHLCV tick, order book snapshot/delta, funding rate), and publishes to partitioned Kafka topics (`market.ticks.<exchange>.<symbol>`, `market.orderbook.<exchange>.<symbol>`). Implement a TimescaleDB writer consumer that persists tick data with hypertable partitioning by `(exchange, symbol, timestamp)`. Target end-to-end latency from exchange WebSocket event to Kafka publish under 10ms.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Kafka topics are created with per-exchange, per-symbol partitioning; each topic retains 7 days of data with log compaction disabled for tick streams
- [ ] Normalized tick events conform to a defined Protobuf schema with fields: `exchange`, `symbol`, `price`, `volume`, `bid`, `ask`, `timestamp_ms`
- [ ] TimescaleDB hypertable `market_ticks` receives all published events via a dedicated Kafka consumer; ingestion lag stays below 500ms under 10,000 msgs/sec load
- [ ] Prometheus metrics expose `market_data_latency_ms` histogram, `kafka_publish_errors_total` counter, and `active_websocket_connections` gauge
- [ ] Load test demonstrates stable throughput at 50,000 market data events/second without memory growth or dropped messages

---

### [E1-S3] WebSocket Gateway for Frontend Streaming
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 3 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, frontend, node.js, websocket

**User Story**
As a retail trader, I want real-time price and order book data pushed to my browser automatically so that I do not need to manually refresh or poll for updates.

**Work Description**
Build the WebSocket Gateway service in Node.js that authenticates client connections via JWT, manages subscriptions to market data topics, and fans out Kafka consumer messages to relevant connected clients. Implement subscription filtering so clients only receive data for symbols they have explicitly subscribed to. Use a Redis pub/sub layer as the intermediary between multiple Gateway pod instances to support horizontal scaling on Kubernetes. Target support for 5,000 concurrent authenticated WebSocket connections per pod.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Clients authenticate the WebSocket handshake with a valid JWT bearer token; unauthenticated connections are rejected with code 4001
- [ ] Clients send `{ "action": "subscribe", "channel": "ticker", "symbol": "BTC/USDT", "exchange": "binance" }` and receive matching events within 50ms of Kafka publish
- [ ] Redis pub/sub fan-out enables multiple Gateway pods to serve the same subscription without each pod independently consuming from Kafka
- [ ] Gateway gracefully handles client disconnects, unsubscribes all associated topics, and releases Redis subscription slots
- [ ] Load test confirms 5,000 concurrent connections per pod with p99 message delivery latency below 80ms and zero message loss

---

### [E1-S4] Paper Trading Simulator
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** medium | **Sprint:** 4 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, python

**User Story**
As a retail trader, I want to execute simulated trades using live market data without risking real funds so that I can learn the platform and validate my approach before committing capital.

**Work Description**
Build the Paper Trading Simulator service in Python that intercepts order requests flagged with `mode: paper`, simulates fill logic using live bid/ask data from the Market Data Hub (including realistic slippage modelling based on order size vs. order book depth), and persists simulated fills to a dedicated `paper_orders` PostgreSQL table. The simulator must expose the same OMS API contract as live trading so the frontend requires zero conditional logic. Simulated portfolio state is tracked in Redis under a namespaced key per user.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Paper orders submitted through the standard OMS REST endpoint with `"mode": "paper"` are routed to the simulator and never forwarded to any exchange adapter
- [ ] Fill simulation uses current best bid/ask from Redis order book snapshot; market orders fill immediately, limit orders fill when simulated price crosses the limit price
- [ ] Slippage is modelled by consuming available order book depth proportional to order size; orders exceeding available depth at a price level are partially filled
- [ ] Simulated portfolio balance, positions, and trade history are queryable via `/api/paper/portfolio` and are isolated per user by `user_id` namespace in Redis
- [ ] Switching between paper and live mode requires an explicit user action via the UI with a confirmation modal; no accidental cross-mode order submission is possible

---

## Epic 2: Order Management System & Smart Order Routing
> Deliver a complete, low-latency order lifecycle management system with intelligent routing to minimize slippage across exchanges.

### [E2-S1] Core Order Management System (OMS)
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** backend, rust, postgresql, redis

**User Story**
As an algorithmic trader, I want all my orders managed through a single system that tracks their full lifecycle so that I have a reliable, auditable record of every trade action taken on my behalf.

**Work Description**
Build the OMS core in Rust using the Tokio async runtime. The OMS must handle order creation, state transitions (NEW → PENDING → PARTIALLY_FILLED → FILLED / CANCELLED / REJECTED), and persistence to PostgreSQL. All state transitions must be published as events to the `orders.events` Kafka topic for downstream consumers (risk engine, portfolio service). Maintain live order state in Redis for sub-millisecond lookups during risk checks. Implement idempotency keys on order submission to prevent duplicate orders on retry.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Order state machine enforces valid transitions only; invalid transition attempts (e.g., FILLED → PENDING) return a 409 error and are logged with full context
- [ ] All order mutations are written to PostgreSQL `orders` table and published to `orders.events` Kafka topic atomically using the outbox pattern
- [ ] Redis holds current order state as a hash keyed by `order:{order_id}` with TTL of 48 hours; Redis and PostgreSQL states reconcile on any discrepancy via a scheduled consistency job
- [ ] Idempotency is enforced via a `client_order_id` unique constraint; duplicate submissions within 24 hours return the original order response with HTTP 200
- [ ] OMS processes 1,000 order state transitions per second under load test with p99 latency below 5ms for Redis reads and below 20ms for PostgreSQL writes

---

### [E2-S2] Order Type Engine (Limit, Stop, OCO, Trailing Stop)
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 3 | **Points:** 10
**Tags:** backend, rust

**User Story**
As a retail trader, I want to place advanced order types including stop-loss, OCO, and trailing stop orders so that I can automate my exit strategies without manually monitoring positions.

**Work Description**
Extend the OMS in Rust with order type handlers for: limit, market, stop-market, stop-limit, OCO (one-cancels-other), and trailing stop. OCO and trailing stop orders are managed as composite orders internally — the OMS monitors live prices via Kafka tick events and triggers child order submissions when conditions are met. All composite order logic runs within the OMS process to avoid distributed coordination latency. Trailing stop must recalculate the trigger price on every tick for active orders.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] OCO orders create two linked child orders; when one child transitions to FILLED or PARTIALLY_FILLED, the sibling is immediately cancelled with reason `OCO_SIBLING_FILLED`
- [ ] Trailing stop orders accept a `trail_amount` (absolute) or `trail_percent` parameter; the stop price updates on every tick that moves the market in the favorable direction but never moves adversely
- [ ] Stop-limit and stop-market orders trigger correctly when the last trade price crosses the stop price; trigger latency from Kafka tick event to child order submission is below 20ms
- [ ] All composite order types are fully represented in the `orders.events` Kafka stream with parent-child relationship fields (`parent_order_id`, `composite_type`)
- [ ] Failure of one leg of an OCO due to exchange rejection triggers an alert event and does not leave the sibling order orphaned; reconciliation logic cancels the orphan within 5 seconds

---

### [E2-S3] Smart Order Router (SOR)
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 3 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** backend, rust

**User Story**
As an institutional portfolio manager, I want large orders automatically split and routed across multiple exchanges to minimize slippage and achieve the best possible execution price.

**Work Description**
Build the Smart Order Router in Rust that receives order requests from the OMS, queries live order book snapshots from Redis for all eligible exchanges, and computes an optimal routing plan that minimizes estimated slippage using a VWAP-based cost model. The SOR splits parent orders into child orders distributed across exchanges and aggregates fill reports back to the parent order in the OMS. Implement routing configuration per user allowing exchange preference, blacklisting, and fee-tier awareness.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] SOR fetches order book snapshots from Redis for all connected exchanges in under 2ms and computes routing plan before any child order is submitted
- [ ] VWAP cost model calculates expected slippage for each exchange based on available liquidity at each price level; routing plan selects the allocation that minimizes total expected cost
- [ ] Child orders are submitted concurrently to target exchanges using async Tokio tasks; total time from routing decision to all child orders submitted is below 50ms
- [ ] Parent order fill percentage is computed as the weighted sum of child fills; parent order transitions to FILLED only when all child orders are filled or the unfilledable remainder is within a configurable tolerance (default 0.1%)
- [ ] SOR routing decisions are logged to the `order_routing_decisions` PostgreSQL table with fields: `order_id`, `exchange_allocations` (JSONB), `estimated_slippage_bps`, `actual_slippage_bps` (populated post-fill)

---

### [E2-S4] API Key Vault Integration & Secure Exchange Credential Management
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, infrastructure, security, node.js

**User Story**
As an institutional portfolio manager, I want my exchange API keys stored and accessed securely so that a platform breach cannot expose my credentials or result in unauthorized trades.

**Work Description**
Integrate HashiCorp Vault into the platform for exchange API key storage. Build an API Key Management service in Node.js that allows users to add, rotate, and revoke exchange API keys through the REST API. Keys are stored in Vault's KV v2 secrets engine under a path scoped to `users/{user_id}/exchanges/{exchange_id}`. The OMS and Exchange Adapters retrieve keys from Vault at request time using short-lived Vault tokens issued via AppRole auth; keys are never written to PostgreSQL or application logs.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Exchange API keys submitted via `POST /api/keys` are written exclusively to Vault KV v2; PostgreSQL stores only a reference record with `vault_path` and key metadata (exchange, label, created_at, ip_whitelist)
- [ ] OMS retrieves exchange credentials from Vault on each order batch using AppRole tokens with TTL of 5 minutes; tokens are cached in-process and refreshed before expiry
- [ ] IP whitelisting is enforced at the application layer: if an outbound exchange API call's source IP is not in the user's configured whitelist, the request is blocked and an alert event is emitted
- [ ] All Vault read/write operations are recorded in the `api_key_audit_log` PostgreSQL table with `user_id`, `action`, `exchange`, `timestamp`, and `request_ip`
- [ ] Key rotation via `PUT /api/keys/{key_id}` atomically writes the new key to Vault and invalidates cached credentials in OMS within 10 seconds via a Redis invalidation signal

---

## Epic 3: Risk Engine, Strategy Engine & Backtesting
> Build the automated strategy execution framework, real-time risk guardrails, and historical backtesting environment.

### [E3-S1] Real-Time Risk Engine
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 3 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** backend, rust, redis, kafka

**User Story**
As an algorithmic trader, I want the platform to enforce configurable risk limits on all my trading activity in real time so that automated strategies cannot exceed my defined loss tolerances or position size caps.

**Work Description**
Build the Risk Engine in Rust as a synchronous gate that evaluates every order submission against configurable risk rules before the OMS routes it to the exchange. Risk state (open positions, daily P&L, drawdown) is maintained in Redis for sub-millisecond reads. Risk rules are loaded from PostgreSQL at startup and cached; rule changes take effect within 5 seconds via a Kafka `risk.config.updated` event. Implement a kill-switch mechanism that halts all order submission for a user or globally.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Every order passes through the Risk Engine synchronously before exchange submission; an order that violates any active rule is rejected with a structured error code (e.g., `RISK_MAX_POSITION_EXCEEDED`) and logged to `risk_rejections` table
- [ ] Supported risk rules: max position size per symbol (USD notional), max open orders count, daily loss limit (USD), max portfolio drawdown (%), per-strategy max allocation (%), and kill-switch (boolean)
- [ ] Risk state in Redis is updated atomically on every fill event consumed from `orders.events` Kafka topic; Redis operations use Lua scripts to ensure read-modify-write atomicity
- [ ] Kill-switch activation via `POST /api/risk/kill-switch` cancels all open orders across all exchanges within 5 seconds and blocks new order submission until explicitly deactivated
- [ ] Risk Engine throughput supports 5,000 order evaluations per second with p99 evaluation latency below 2ms under load test
