# MailForge AI — Product Requirements Document

> **Version:** 1.0.0 | **Status:** Ready for Stakeholder Review | **Last Updated:** 2025 | **Owner:** Forge Platform

---

## Executive Summary

MailForge AI is an intelligent email marketing platform that transforms how businesses of all sizes create, personalize, and optimize their email campaigns. By harnessing the power of large language models — specifically Anthropic's Claude — alongside purpose-built machine learning models for behavioral prediction, MailForge AI eliminates the primary bottleneck of modern email marketing: the gap between what marketers want to say and their capacity to say it well, consistently, and at scale. The platform covers the entire email marketing lifecycle from first draft to final performance report, replacing what was once a fragmented, skill-intensive workflow with a single, cohesive AI-assisted workspace.

The market opportunity is substantial and underserved. Small business owners lack the budget for dedicated copywriters. Digital marketing managers are drowning in segmentation complexity. E-commerce operators need personalized, behavior-triggered content updating on a daily cadence. All of these users are currently served by tools that provide the delivery infrastructure but leave the hardest parts — writing compelling copy, identifying the right audience, knowing when to send — entirely to the user. MailForge AI inverts this model: the platform does the heavy cognitive lifting while the user provides strategic direction and brand judgment.

MailForge AI is architected as a cloud-native, API-first microservices platform deployed on AWS, engineered for enterprise-grade scale (up to one million subscribers), and designed to meet global regulatory requirements including GDPR, CAN-SPAM, and CASL. The product is built to be the last email marketing tool a growing business needs — capable of serving a sole proprietor launching their first newsletter on day one, and a marketing team managing complex multi-segment drip campaigns on day one thousand.

---

## The Problem

Email marketing remains one of the highest-ROI digital marketing channels, with industry studies consistently reporting returns of $36–$42 per dollar spent. Yet the majority of businesses — particularly those without large, dedicated marketing teams — consistently underperform against this potential. The root cause is not a lack of access to delivery infrastructure; ESPs like Mailchimp, SendGrid, and Klaviyo are widely adopted and relatively affordable. The root cause is a **content and intelligence gap**. Businesses can get their emails into inboxes; they simply do not know what to write, who to write it for, or when to send it.

Producing high-quality, personalized email content at scale requires a combination of copywriting expertise, audience behavioral analysis, data interpretation skills, and A/B testing discipline that most teams do not possess simultaneously. A small business owner writing their own campaigns is context-switching out of their core domain. A marketing manager managing ten audience segments is manually copying and adjusting copy blocks across variants. A copywriter produces excellent prose but lacks the analytics feedback loop to know which choices actually drive clicks. The result is a predictable pattern of generic batch-and-blast emails, low open rates, subscriber fatigue, and eventual list decay. Existing tools provide no meaningful assistance with any of these problems — they are pipes, not partners.

The consequences are measurable and costly: industry average open rates hover around 21%, click-through rates around 2.5%, and unsubscribe rates climb as audiences receive irrelevant content. Beyond the direct performance deficit, there is a compounding opportunity cost — every hour a business owner or marketer spends wrestling with email copy is an hour not spent on strategy, product, or customer relationships. MailForge AI is designed to eliminate this entire class of problem.

---

## The Problem

### Core Pain Points at a Glance

| Pain Area | Who Feels It | Business Impact |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| Content creation bottleneck | All user segments | Campaigns delayed, fewer sends, stale messaging |
| Generic, non-personalized copy | Small Businesses, E-commerce | Low open rates, high unsubscribes |
| Manual segmentation complexity | Marketing Managers | Hours lost per campaign, errors at scale |
| No data-driven content guidance | Copywriters, Small Businesses | Decisions made by intuition, not evidence |
| Fragmented toolchain | All user segments | Context switching, integration overhead, data silos |
| Deliverability blind spots | E-commerce, Marketing Managers | Campaigns landing in spam, damaged sender reputation |

---

## The Solution

MailForge AI solves the content and intelligence gap through three interlocking capabilities that no existing email tool combines effectively.

**First, generative AI content production.** Using Claude Opus for deep email copy generation and Claude Haiku for real-time scoring and suggestions, MailForge AI can produce complete, on-brand email campaigns — subject lines, preheader text, body copy, and CTAs — in under five seconds. The system is not a generic text generator; it is contextualized by each user's defined brand voice profile, stored as vector embeddings in Pinecone, and grounded in industry-specific benchmarks and the user's own historical campaign performance.

**Second, behavioral intelligence and optimization.** MailForge AI's ML layer analyzes subscriber engagement history to predict optimal send times at the individual-segment level, automatically surfaces A/B test winners, and powers an audience segmentation engine that dynamically personalizes content based on demographics, purchase behavior, and engagement scoring — capabilities that previously required a dedicated marketing data analyst.

**Third, end-to-end workflow integration.** Rather than being a content tool that exports into another system, MailForge AI manages the entire campaign lifecycle: from template design in a drag-and-drop builder, through scheduling and multi-ESP delivery with automatic failover, to real-time analytics in a unified ClickHouse-backed dashboard with AI-generated improvement recommendations. Integrations with HubSpot, Salesforce, Shopify, and WooCommerce ensure that MailForge AI operates as the intelligent orchestration layer on top of a business's existing commercial stack, not a replacement that requires data migration.

---

## User Personas

### Maya Chen — Small Business Owner

> *"I know my customers. I just don't have time to write five emails a week telling them that."*

**Background:** Maya owns a specialty tea subscription box with ~2,400 email subscribers. She handles operations, social media, and customer support herself with one part-time employee. Email marketing is critically important to her revenue but perpetually deprioritized due to time constraints.

**Goals:**
- Launch and schedule email campaigns in under 30 minutes per week
- Grow subscriber engagement and repeat purchase rate without hiring a copywriter
- Understand which emails are actually driving sales vs. just opens
- Maintain a consistent, warm brand voice across all communications

**Pain Points:**
- No dedicated marketing team or budget for professional copywriters
- Spends 3–4 hours per campaign writing, second-guessing, and formatting
- Cannot identify why some emails perform dramatically better than others
- Generic templates from current ESP feel off-brand and impersonal

**How MailForge AI Helps:**
Maya sets up her brand voice profile once — uploading past emails she liked, defining her tone as "warm, knowledgeable, never corporate" — and MailForge AI generates on-brand campaign drafts in seconds. The send-time optimizer ensures her campaigns hit inboxes when her subscribers are most likely reading. The analytics dashboard surfaced in plain language tells her that "product education emails get 2x the clicks of promotional emails" — informing her strategy without requiring data literacy. Her average campaign creation time drops from 3+ hours to under 25 minutes.

---

### Jordan Rivera — Digital Marketing Manager

> *"I'm managing 14 audience segments across 3 product lines. My biggest problem isn't creativity — it's capacity."*

**Background:** Jordan is a marketing manager at a B2B SaaS company with a subscriber list of 85,000 contacts segmented by industry vertical, lifecycle stage, and product usage tier. They manage two junior marketers and are directly accountable for email-driven pipeline contribution.

**Goals:**
- Scale personalized content across all segments without proportionally scaling headcount
- Demonstrate measurable email-attributed revenue to leadership
- Run rigorous A/B tests without manual winner selection and redeployment
- Reduce campaign production cycle from 2 weeks to 3–4 days

**Pain Points:**
- Manually adapting copy for each audience segment is a full-time job in itself
- A/B testing is manual: run test, wait, export data, analyze, re-upload winner
- Current analytics tools require BI team involvement to produce executive reports
- Pressure to deliver 20%+ YoY improvement in email-driven pipeline

**How MailForge AI Helps:**
Jordan configures the Audience Segmentation Engine with behavioral and firmographic rules, and the AI Content Generator produces segment-specific copy variations automatically — the "enterprise security buyer" variant emphasizes compliance; the "startup" variant leads with speed-to-value. A/B Testing Automation handles the entire test lifecycle, automatically deploying winners to remaining audiences. The Analytics Dashboard provides revenue attribution reports exportable to PDF for leadership presentations. Jordan's team now ships more campaigns per week with greater consistency than before.

---

### Priya Nair — E-commerce Store Owner

> *"I need abandoned cart emails updating every time I change my product catalog. That's 40 emails I have to rewrite manually."*

**Background:** Priya runs a mid-sized online skincare store on Shopify with 28,000 subscribers. She relies heavily on automated email sequences — welcome series, abandoned cart recovery, post-purchase follow-ups, and seasonal promotions — and loses significant revenue when these sequences become stale or misconfigured.

**Goals:**
- Keep automated email sequences current with product catalog changes automatically
- Dramatically reduce time-to-launch for seasonal promotional campaigns
- Personalize product recommendations in emails based on purchase history
- Eliminate spam complaints and improve inbox delivery rates

**Pain Points:**
- Abandoned cart and re-engagement emails require constant manual updating
- Seasonal campaigns (Black Friday, Valentine's Day) demand rapid content turnaround under peak operational pressure
- Poor segmentation means subscribers receive promotions for products they already purchased
- Deliverability issues cost her estimated $4,000/month in lost cart recovery revenue

**How MailForge AI Helps:**
The Shopify integration syncs Priya's product catalog automatically, enabling the Personalization Engine to inject current product names, prices, and images into email templates dynamically. Abandoned cart sequences regenerate copy variations for each product category using AI, ensuring relevance without manual effort. The Spam and Deliverability Checker flags issues before send, and the List Health Monitor automatically suppresses disengaged contacts to protect sender reputation. Her cart recovery email deliverability improves from 87% to 96%, directly recovering revenue she was leaving on the table.

---

### Alex Morgan — Content Creator / Copywriter

> *"I love writing. What I hate is writing the same promotional email seventeen slightly different ways for seventeen different segments."*

**Background:** Alex is a freelance copywriter who manages email marketing for four B2C clients simultaneously. They are skilled at crafting compelling narrative copy but spend a disproportionate amount of time on mechanical adaptation, revision cycles, and client approval management.

**Goals:**
- Use AI as a force multiplier for creative output, not a replacement for voice
- Maintain distinct brand voice consistency for each client without cognitive overhead
- Access performance data to make data-informed creative decisions
- Streamline the revision and client approval process

**Pain Points:**
- Writer's block hits hardest on the 12th subject line variation of the day
- Maintaining four distinct client brand voices simultaneously leads to cross-contamination errors
- No visibility into which creative choices — specific phrases, CTA styles, emotional tones — actually drive performance
- Revision workflows involve emailing Word documents back and forth with tracked changes

**How MailForge AI Helps:**
Alex configures a separate Brand Voice Profile for each client, stored in Pinecone so the AI's outputs are semantically anchored to each brand's unique style. The AI Content Generator handles first drafts and mechanical variations, freeing Alex to focus on creative refinement and strategic storytelling. Performance analytics close the feedback loop — Alex can see that a conversational opener outperforms a headline opener for Client A, informing future creative decisions with evidence. The platform's collaboration features streamline client review without document sprawl.

---

## Epic 1: Platform Foundation & Authentication
> Establish the core infrastructure, API gateway, authentication system, and developer environment to support all downstream microservices.

### [E1-S1] Project Scaffolding and Local Development Environment
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** infrastructure, backend, devops

**User Story**
As a developer, I want a fully configured local development environment so that I can build and test all microservices without manual infrastructure setup.

**Work Description**
Scaffold the monorepo structure with workspaces for all 7 microservices (Auth, AI Content, Campaign, Analytics, List Management, Delivery, Integration) plus the React frontend. Configure Docker Compose to spin up all backing services including PostgreSQL 15, Redis 7, ClickHouse, and a local Kafka broker. Set up shared Zod schema packages, ESLint/Prettier configs, and a Makefile with one-command startup.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] `make dev` starts all services and backing infrastructure with no manual steps required
- [ ] All 7 backend services boot without errors and pass health check endpoints at their designated ports
- [ ] Shared TypeScript types and Zod schema package is importable across all Node.js services
- [ ] Docker Compose includes PostgreSQL, Redis, ClickHouse, Kafka (via Redpanda local image), and all app containers
- [ ] README documents full local setup steps including prerequisite versions and `.env.example` file is present for all services

---

### [E1-S2] Auth Service — JWT and OAuth2 Authentication
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, security

**User Story**
As a user, I want to register, log in, and authenticate via Google or email/password so that I can securely access the platform.

**Work Description**
Build the Auth Service using Node.js 20 + Fastify with Passport.js handling OAuth2 (Google) and local strategy. Implement JWT issuance with 15-minute access tokens and 7-day refresh tokens stored in Redis. Prisma schema must define the `users`, `organizations`, `roles`, and `sessions` tables in PostgreSQL. Expose `/auth/register`, `/auth/login`, `/auth/oauth/google`, `/auth/refresh`, and `/auth/logout` endpoints with full OpenAPI 3.0 spec via Fastify's schema validation.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Users can register with email/password and receive a verified JWT access token and refresh token
- [ ] Google OAuth2 flow completes and creates or links a user account, returning valid tokens
- [ ] Expired access tokens are rejected with `401`; valid refresh tokens correctly issue new access tokens
- [ ] Role-based middleware (`owner`, `admin`, `editor`, `viewer`) blocks unauthorized routes with `403`
- [ ] All auth endpoints are documented in auto-generated OpenAPI 3.0 spec accessible at `/docs`
- [ ] Passwords are hashed with bcrypt (min cost factor 12); plaintext passwords never stored or logged

---

### [E1-S3] API Gateway, Rate Limiting, and Service Routing
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 5
**Tags:** backend, infrastructure

**User Story**
As a developer, I want a central API gateway that routes requests to the correct microservice so that the frontend communicates through a single, secure entry point.

**Work Description**
Configure the Fastify API Gateway service as the single entry point for all client traffic. Implement reverse-proxy routing to each downstream microservice using `@fastify/http-proxy`. Add JWT verification middleware that validates tokens on all protected routes before forwarding. Implement per-user rate limiting via Redis (100 req/min standard tier, 500 req/min pro tier) using `@fastify/rate-limit`. Propagate `X-User-ID` and `X-Org-ID` headers to downstream services.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] All 7 service route prefixes (`/api/content`, `/api/campaigns`, `/api/analytics`, `/api/lists`, `/api/delivery`, `/api/integrations`, `/api/auth`) are correctly proxied to their respective services
- [ ] Requests without a valid JWT to protected routes return `401` before reaching downstream services
- [ ] Rate limiting correctly enforces per-user limits and returns `429` with `Retry-After` header when exceeded
- [ ] `X-User-ID` and `X-Org-ID` headers are injected into every forwarded request based on the decoded JWT
- [ ] Gateway health endpoint `/health` returns status of all downstream services with individual up/down indicators

---

### [E1-S4] CI/CD Pipeline and Terraform Infrastructure Baseline
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** devops, infrastructure

**User Story**
As a developer, I want automated testing and deployment pipelines so that code changes are validated and deployed to AWS without manual intervention.

**Work Description**
Write GitHub Actions workflows for each service: on PR, run lint, type-check, and unit tests; on merge to `main`, build Docker images, push to ECR, and deploy to ECS Fargate staging. Write Terraform modules for AWS ECS Fargate cluster, RDS Aurora PostgreSQL, ElastiCache Redis, MSK Kafka, S3 buckets, CloudFront distribution, and Secrets Manager. Staging and production workspaces must be isolated via Terraform workspaces.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] PR pipeline runs lint, TypeScript type-check, and unit tests for all affected services; failing checks block merge
- [ ] Merging to `main` triggers Docker build, ECR push, and zero-downtime ECS rolling deployment to staging within 8 minutes
- [ ] Terraform `plan` and `apply` are gated behind manual approval in the production GitHub Actions environment
- [ ] All secrets (DB credentials, API keys, JWT secret) are stored in AWS Secrets Manager and injected as ECS task environment variables — no secrets in code or Docker images
- [ ] Datadog agent is deployed as an ECS sidecar container and emits service health metrics to the Datadog dashboard

---

## Epic 2: AI Content Generation and Brand Voice
> Deliver the AI-powered email content generation engine, subject line optimizer, brand voice profiles, and multi-language support that form the core product value proposition.

### [E2-S1] AI Content Service — Email Copy Generation with Claude
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** backend, ai-ml

**User Story**
As a small business owner, I want to generate full email copy by describing my goal so that I can produce professional campaigns without a copywriter.

**Work Description**
Build the AI Content Service in Python 3.12 + FastAPI. Implement a `/generate/email` endpoint that accepts `goal`, `tone`, `industry`, `content_type` (promotional, newsletter, transactional, re-engagement), and `audience_description` as inputs. Use LangChain to orchestrate prompt assembly — inject brand voice profile from Pinecone vector similarity search (top-3 relevant past campaigns) into the system prompt. Route requests to Claude Opus for body copy generation. Enforce a 5-second timeout with graceful streaming fallback via Server-Sent Events. Log token usage per request to PostgreSQL for billing.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] `POST /generate/email` returns subject line, preheader text, full body HTML, and at least 2 CTA variations within 5 seconds for 95th percentile requests
- [ ] All 4 content types (promotional, newsletter, transactional, re-engagement) produce structurally and tonally distinct output verified by a test suite with Claude-as-judge evaluation
- [ ] Brand voice context from Pinecone is retrieved and injected into the prompt when a brand profile exists for the organization; no brand profile results in a neutral tone fallback
- [ ] Token usage (input + output tokens) per generation request is persisted to the `ai_usage_logs` table with `org_id`, `user_id`, `model`, and timestamp
- [ ] API responds with `503` and a user-friendly error message if Claude API latency exceeds 5 seconds; partial streaming output is preserved when SSE is active

---

### [E2-S2] Subject Line Optimizer with Claude Haiku
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 3 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, ai-ml

**User Story**
As a digital marketing manager, I want AI-scored subject line variations so that I can choose the option most likely to achieve a high open rate.

**Work Description**
Implement `/generate/subject-lines` endpoint in the AI Content Service. Use Claude Haiku to generate 5–10 subject line variations per request and score each on: predicted open rate (0–100), emotional tone label, character length, spam risk score (0–10), and an emoji recommendation flag. Scoring must complete in under 1 second. Store all generated variations and scores in PostgreSQL under the campaign's content history for A/B test seeding. Return variations ranked by predicted open rate descending.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Endpoint returns a minimum of 5 and maximum of 10 subject line variations with scores for all 5 dimensions within 1 second at p95
- [ ] Spam risk score flags any subject line containing known spam trigger words (loaded from a maintained keyword list) with a score ≥ 7 and a warning label
- [ ] Variations with character length outside 30–60 characters are flagged with a `length_warning` field in the response
- [ ] All generated subject lines and their scores are persisted to `subject_line_history` table linked to `campaign_id` for later A/B test use
- [ ] Endpoint accepts an optional `tone_preference` enum (`urgent`, `friendly`, `professional`, `curious`) and demonstrably biases output toward that tone

---

### [E2-S3] Brand Voice Profile Setup and Pinecone Vectorization
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** medium | **Sprint:** 3 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, ai-ml, frontend

**User Story**
As a content creator, I want to define my brand's tone and vocabulary so that every AI-generated email sounds like it was written by our team.

**Work Description**
Build a brand voice onboarding flow: frontend form collects `brand_name`, `tone_adjectives` (multi-select: professional, playful, empathetic, bold, etc.), `words_to_avoid`, `preferred_vocabulary`, and optionally 1–3 sample email pastes. Backend stores the structured profile in PostgreSQL and generates OpenAI text-embedding-3-small embeddings for each sample email and the combined vocabulary description. Upsert embeddings to Pinecone under the organization's namespace. Expose a `/brand-profile/update` endpoint that re-vectorizes on change.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Brand voice profile form validates that at least 2 tone adjectives are selected and at least 1 sample email or 5 vocabulary preferences are provided before saving
- [ ] On save, embeddings for all sample emails and the vocabulary blob are upserted to Pinecone within 10 seconds with the correct `org_id` namespace
- [ ] Updating the brand profile triggers re-embedding and Pinecone upsert for changed fields only; unchanged embeddings are not re-processed
- [ ] Generated email content for an org with a brand profile demonstrably avoids words in the `words_to_avoid` list — validated by an automated test that checks Claude output against the list
- [ ] Brand profile is displayed on a settings page showing all configured preferences with inline edit capability

---

### [E2-S4] Multi-Language Content Generation
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** low | **Sprint:** 9 | **Points:** 5
**Tags:** backend, ai-ml, frontend

**User Story**
As an e-commerce store owner, I want to generate email campaigns in multiple languages so that I can engage my international customer segments without hiring translators.

**Work Description**
Extend the `/generate/email` endpoint to accept a `target_language` parameter supporting ISO 639-1 codes for 10 initial languages (en, es, fr, de, pt, it, nl, ja, zh, ar). Pass language and region context to Claude Opus in the system prompt, instructing culturally appropriate tone adjustments (e.g., formal register for German business email, high-context communication for Japanese). Add a language selector to the frontend content generation form. RTL language support (Arabic) must be applied to the email template builder output via HTML `dir="rtl"` attribute.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] `target_language` parameter accepts all 10 specified ISO 639-1 codes; unsupported codes return a `400` with a list of supported languages
- [ ] Generated content for each supported language is evaluated by a native-speaker prompt (Claude-as-judge) to confirm grammatical correctness and cultural appropriateness in the test suite
- [ ] Arabic and Hebrew outputs render correctly in the email template builder with `dir="rtl"` applied to the email HTML root element
- [ ] The language selector in the frontend shows the user's 5 most recently used languages at the top of the dropdown
- [ ] Token usage logs include the `target_language` field for cost attribution per language

---

## Epic 3: Campaign Management, Template Builder, and Delivery
> Build the drag-and-drop template editor, audience segmentation, campaign scheduling with send-time optimization, and the multi-ESP delivery layer.

### [E3-S1] Drag-and-Drop Email Template Builder with GrapeJS and MJML
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** frontend, backend

**User Story**
As a small business owner, I want to design professional email templates visually so that I can create branded emails without writing any code.

**Work Description**
Integrate GrapeJS into the React frontend as an isolated route `/templates/editor`. Configure a custom GrapeJS block library with 12+ pre-built email blocks (hero, text, image, CTA button, divider, social links, product card, footer). Build a custom GrapeJS-to-MJML export plugin that converts the canvas JSON to valid MJML 4.x, then compiles MJML to production HTML on the backend Template Service endpoint `POST /templates/compile`. Store template JSON and compiled HTML in PostgreSQL; store image assets in S3 with CloudFront URLs. Provide 20+ industry-specific starter templates loaded from S3 on template creation.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Users can drag, drop, resize, and reorder all 12 block types on the GrapeJS canvas without browser console errors
- [ ] Compiled MJML output renders identically (pixel-level comparison via Playwright screenshot diff < 5% delta) in Gmail web, Outlook 2019, and Apple Mail
- [ ] Template JSON is auto-saved to the backend every 30 seconds while the editor is open; unsaved changes trigger a browser leave-page confirmation dialog
- [ ] Image uploads from the editor are stored in S3, served via CloudFront, and the CloudFront URL is injected into the MJML `<mj-image>` src attribute
- [ ] All 20 starter templates load in the template picker within 1 second using pre-signed S3 URLs with CloudFront caching

---

### [E3-S2] Audience Segmentation Engine and List Management Service
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 4 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** backend, frontend

**User Story**
As a digital marketing manager, I want to create audience segments using behavioral and demographic filters so that I can send targeted emails that convert better.

**Work Description**
Build the List Management Service (Node.js + Fastify + Prisma). Define the `subscribers`, `lists`, `segments`, and `segment_rules` tables in PostgreSQL. Implement a rules-based segment builder that supports AND/OR filter groups on subscriber attributes: email domain, tags, country, signup date, last opened, last clicked, total purchases, and custom properties (JSONB). Segment evaluation must run as a BullMQ background job and cache the resulting subscriber ID set in Redis with a 15-minute TTL. Frontend segment builder must use a visual rule-group UI (similar to Mailchimp segments). List import via CSV (up to 500K rows) processed via S3 upload + BullMQ worker.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Segment rule builder supports at minimum: string (equals, contains, starts with), numeric (greater than, less than, between), date (before, after, within last N days), and boolean filter operators
- [ ] Segment evaluation for a list of 1 million subscribers completes within 30 seconds as a background BullMQ job; job progress is streamed to the frontend via WebSocket
- [ ] CSV import of 500,000 rows completes without timeout — uploaded to S3, processed by a BullMQ worker, and duplicate emails are detected and merged rather than creating new records
- [ ] Invalid email addresses

# CursorLens — Product Requirements Document

> **Prepared by:** Press, Documentation Agent — Forge Platform
> **Status:** Draft v1.0 — Ready for Stakeholder Review
> **Estimated Build Cost:** $3,200–$6,800/month infrastructure | **Timeline:** 16 weeks | **Complexity:** High

---

## Executive Summary

Engineering organizations are making significant investments in AI-assisted development tooling — yet they are flying blind. Cursor IDE has rapidly become one of the most powerful AI coding environments available, but adoption alone does not guarantee productivity gains. Without measurement, teams cannot distinguish developers who are thriving with AI assistance from those who are barely using it, cannot justify license renewals to finance teams, and cannot identify where coaching or workflow changes would have the highest impact. CursorLens closes this gap.

CursorLens is a developer analytics platform purpose-built for Cursor IDE — the first of its kind to turn raw IDE telemetry into actionable productivity intelligence. By deploying a lightweight background extension alongside a real-time analytics backend, CursorLens captures the full spectrum of AI-assisted coding activity: autocomplete acceptances, Composer interactions, chat queries, code generation events, and manual edits. This raw signal is transformed — in seconds — into meaningful metrics: time saved estimates, AI feature utilization heatmaps, acceptance rate trends, and team-wide benchmarks. The result is a living, breathing productivity record that grows more valuable with every coding session.

The platform is designed for three distinct audiences operating at different altitudes. Individual developers gain a personal productivity mirror — a feedback loop that helps them improve their prompting strategies and identify untapped Cursor features. Engineering managers gain the ROI visibility they need to defend tooling budgets and identify who needs support. DevOps and platform engineers gain a centralized analytics layer that can be wired directly into existing engineering metrics pipelines. CursorLens is not a surveillance tool — it is a coaching platform, built privacy-first, where developer trust is treated as a first-class architectural requirement alongside performance and scalability.

---

## The Problem

Organizations adopting Cursor IDE face a fundamental measurement gap: they can see their license invoices clearly, but they cannot see their returns. AI-assisted coding is not self-evidently productive — its value depends heavily on how individual developers engage with features, the quality of their prompts, their awareness of available capabilities, and whether workflows have been optimized around AI collaboration. None of this is visible without instrumentation.

This creates cascading problems across organizational levels. Engineering managers cannot generate credible ROI reports for finance or leadership without manual data collection — an error-prone and time-consuming process that most teams simply do not do. Developers themselves lack a feedback loop: they have no way to know whether their AI suggestion acceptance rate is improving, which features they are underusing, or whether their prompting strategies are effective. Meanwhile, platform and DevOps engineers who are responsible for holistic engineering metrics have no integration path — Cursor IDE usage data lives in a black box, disconnected from commit frequency, PR cycle time, deployment velocity, and the other signals that paint a complete picture of developer effectiveness.

The absence of data does not just make measurement hard — it makes optimization impossible. Teams cannot improve what they cannot see. Without CursorLens, organizations are making multi-thousand-dollar AI tooling investments based on intuition, anecdote, and occasional developer surveys, leaving substantial productivity potential unrealized and making every license renewal a leap of faith.

---

## The Solution

CursorLens solves the developer AI measurement problem through a three-layer architecture: a zero-friction IDE extension that captures high-frequency telemetry with less than 1% CPU overhead, an event-driven processing pipeline that transforms raw events into meaningful metrics in near real-time, and a role-aware dashboard that surfaces exactly the right insights to each type of user without data overload.

What makes CursorLens uniquely positioned is its combination of granularity and trust. The platform captures the signal that no other tool collects — AI-specific interactions inside Cursor IDE — while being designed from the ground up to earn developer confidence through opt-in data collection, pseudonymization at ingestion, and privacy controls that give individuals genuine ownership of their data. Team views enforce minimum cohort sizes to prevent surveillance dynamics, and a dedicated Privacy & Governance Service ensures compliance controls are a first-class architectural concern rather than a bolted-on afterthought.

CursorLens also connects the IDE to the broader engineering ecosystem. Integrations with GitHub, GitLab, Jira, and Linear allow the platform to correlate Cursor usage with commit frequency, PR cycle times, and ticket resolution speed — producing a holistic view of how AI-assisted coding translates into actual delivery outcomes. Combined with Claude-powered prompt analysis that surfaces personalized recommendations, CursorLens becomes not just a measurement platform but a continuous improvement engine — turning data into action at both the individual and organizational level.

---

## User Personas

### Alex Chen — Individual Developer

**Goals:**
- Understand how their productivity has changed since adopting Cursor IDE with objective, personal data
- Identify which Cursor AI features they are not using and learn how to integrate them into daily workflows
- Improve the quality and effectiveness of their AI prompts to get better suggestions on the first try
- Track personal productivity streaks and milestones to maintain motivation around AI tool adoption

**Pain Points:**
- Has no way to objectively measure their own productivity improvement over time — progress feels anecdotal
- Is unaware that they are barely using Composer or Edit mode, relying almost exclusively on autocomplete
- Has no baseline to compare AI-assisted sessions against manual coding sessions from six months ago
- Keeps getting low-quality suggestions but doesn't know whether the problem is their prompts or the tool itself

**How CursorLens Helps:**
Alex opens their personal dashboard each morning to see yesterday's acceptance rate, time-saved estimate, and a feature utilization heatmap showing that Composer is sitting at 4% usage. The Prompt Effectiveness Analyzer flags that Alex's prompts tend to be under 10 words and suggests adding context about the target function signature. Within 60 days, Alex's acceptance rate has climbed from 31% to 49% — a number they can share in their next performance review with confidence.

---

### Morgan Lee — Engineering Manager

**Goals:**
- Generate credible, export-ready ROI reports demonstrating the value of Cursor IDE investment to leadership and finance
- Identify developers on the team who have low adoption rates and need additional training or onboarding support
- Establish team-wide productivity benchmarks and track improvement trends across quarterly planning cycles
- Make data-backed tooling decisions rather than relying on developer surveys or gut feel

**Pain Points:**
- Cannot produce an ROI report without spending hours manually collecting anecdotal data from developers
- Has no visibility into whether the 12 new engineers onboarded last quarter have actually adopted Cursor AI features
- Struggles to identify which developers would benefit most from a Cursor workflow workshop
- Lacks the data artifacts needed to support a Cursor license expansion request in the next planning meeting

**How CursorLens Helps:**
Morgan opens the Team Analytics view and can immediately see a ranked list of team members by AI adoption score, identify the three developers sitting below the team average, and schedule targeted check-ins. Before the quarterly business review, Morgan clicks "Generate Report," selects a date range, and downloads a polished PDF comparing baseline metrics from onboarding to current performance — ready for the presentation in under five minutes, without involving a data analyst.

---

### Jordan Rivera — DevOps / Platform Engineer

**Goals:**
- Integrate developer productivity telemetry from Cursor IDE into the existing engineering metrics pipeline alongside deployment frequency and incident rates
- Build a cross-team view of AI tool adoption to inform platform tooling strategy and support organizational standardization decisions
- Correlate IDE usage patterns with downstream delivery quality to quantify the end-to-end impact of AI-assisted development

**Pain Points:**
- Cursor IDE usage data is siloed and cannot be piped into Datadog, Grafana, or internal metrics dashboards without custom scraping work
- Cannot tell whether high Cursor usage on a team correlates with fewer bugs, faster PRs, or improved deployment frequency
- Responsible for recommending developer tooling standards across multiple teams but has no cross-team data to base recommendations on

**How CursorLens Helps:**
Jordan connects CursorLens to the organization's GitHub and Jira instances through the Integration Service. Within a week, the DevOps view surfaces a correlation view showing that the three teams with the highest Cursor AI acceptance rates also have 23% shorter PR cycle times. Jordan exports this data via the API into the internal Grafana stack and uses it to build the case for a company-wide Cursor IDE standardization proposal.

---

## Feature Requirements

### MVP — Phase 1 (Weeks 1–10)

| Feature | Description | Priority | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cursor IDE Activity Tracker | Lightweight VS Code API extension capturing autocomplete acceptances, chat interactions, code generation events, and manual edits with 5-second batched transmission and <1% CPU overhead | High | XL |
| Event Ingestion Pipeline | API Gateway → Auth Service → Kafka event bus with batching, compression, and durable replay; raw event archival to S3 | High | XL |
| Stream Processing Layer | Apache Flink consumers transforming raw Kafka events into aggregated metrics written to TimescaleDB and PostgreSQL | High | XL |
| Productivity Metrics Dashboard | Developer-facing view displaying acceptance rate, lines AI-generated vs manual, estimated time saved, session duration, and daily/weekly trend charts | High | L |
| AI Feature Utilization Heatmap | Visual breakdown of usage frequency across Cursor features (Autocomplete, Composer, Chat, Edit Mode) with effectiveness scores per feature | High | M |
| Team Analytics & Benchmarking | Manager-facing aggregated view of team productivity trends, adoption rankings, and benchmark comparisons with privacy-enforced minimum cohort floors | High | L |
| Productivity Improvement Reports | Automated weekly/monthly reports comparing baseline to current metrics; PDF and CSV export via Puppeteer and fast-csv within a dedicated Report Service | High | L |
| Privacy & Data Governance Controls | Developer opt-in/opt-out controls, pseudonymization at ingestion with rotating IDs, data retention policy management, and RBAC enforcement across all views | High | L |
| Auth & RBAC Service | Role-based access control supporting Developer, Manager, and DevOps roles with appropriate data access scopes enforced at the GraphQL API layer | High | M |
| GraphQL API Layer | Apollo Server exposing unified GraphQL API with role-aware resolvers, subscriptions for real-time dashboard updates, and schema designed for three consumer personas | High | L |
| React Dashboard — Next.js | Next.js 14 frontend with role-specific views (Developer, Manager, DevOps), TanStack Query for data fetching, Recharts and D3.js for visualizations, shadcn/ui components | High | XL |

---

### Phase 2 (Weeks 11–16 and Beyond)

| Feature | Description | Priority | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| Goal Setting & Progress Tracking | Developer and manager-defined productivity goals (e.g., "increase acceptance rate by 20%") with milestone tracking, streak notifications, and progress visualizations | Medium | M |
| Integration with Developer Toolchain | Pre-built connectors to GitHub, GitLab, Jira, and Linear to correlate Cursor IDE metrics with commit frequency, PR cycle time, and ticket resolution speed | Medium | XL |
| Prompt Effectiveness Analyzer | Claude API-powered analysis of prompt patterns correlated with acceptance vs rejection outcomes; generates personalized, actionable prompting recommendations per developer | Medium | L |
| Onboarding & Adoption Coaching | Role-aware personalized onboarding flows, in-app feature discovery tips, and training recommendations triggered by usage pattern analysis for new Cursor IDE users | Low | M |
| On-Premise Kubernetes Deployment Bundle | Helm chart packaging of the full platform stack for enterprise customers with data residency requirements; feature-parity with cloud deployment | High (enterprise) | XL |
| API Export & Webhook Support | REST and webhook endpoints allowing DevOps teams to pipe CursorLens metrics into external observability platforms (Datadog, Grafana, custom pipelines) | Medium | M |
| Advanced Correlation Analytics | Statistical correlation views between Cursor AI adoption metrics and downstream outcomes (deployment frequency, defect rate, PR merge time) in the DevOps view | Medium | L |

---

## Technical Requirements

### Architecture
CursorLens is built on an **event-driven architecture** using Apache Kafka as the central event bus, enabling decoupled, durable, and replay-capable ingestion of high-frequency IDE telemetry. Apache Flink handles sub-second stream processing, writing aggregated results to TimescaleDB (time-series metrics) and PostgreSQL (relational analytics). The GraphQL API layer (Apollo Server) serves three role-differentiated frontend views built in Next.js 14.

```
Cursor IDE Extension → API Gateway → Auth & RBAC Service → Event Ingestion Service
    → Kafka Event Bus → [Stream Processor (Flink) | Raw Store (S3)]
    → [TimescaleDB | PostgreSQL | Redis Cache]
    → Metrics & Aggregation Service → [Report Service | Goal Service | Prompt Service]
    → GraphQL API → Next.js Dashboard → [Developer View | Manager View | DevOps View]
```

### Performance
- **Dashboard load time:** Under 10 seconds for productivity reports across up to 100 developers, achieved via TimescaleDB continuous aggregates and Redis caching of pre-computed metrics
- **IDE extension overhead:** Less than 1% CPU impact enforced through local 5-second event batching with compressed payloads — no per-keystroke network calls
- **Report generation:** ROI reports generated and exported in under 5 minutes via pre-scheduled aggregation in the Report Service — generation is a render operation, not a live query
- **API response targets:** GraphQL query P95 latency under 500ms for dashboard queries; real-time subscriptions for live metric updates without polling
- **Event ingestion throughput:** Pipeline must sustain high-frequency telemetry bursts from concurrent developer sessions without back-pressure or event loss

### Security
- **Encryption in transit:** End-to-end TLS encryption for all telemetry transmitted from the IDE extension to the API Gateway
- **Encryption at rest:** AWS KMS-managed encryption for all data stored in TimescaleDB, PostgreSQL, S3, and Redis
- **Pseudonymization:** Developer identities are pseudonymized with rotating IDs at the Event Ingestion Service before any data reaches persistent storage — raw identifiers never enter the database layer
- **RBAC enforcement:** Role-based access control enforced at the GraphQL resolver layer; Developers access only their own data, Managers access team aggregates, DevOps accesses cross-team views — all with minimum cohort size floors
- **Data residency:** On-premise Kubernetes deployment option for enterprise customers who cannot transmit telemetry outside their network perimeter
- **Audit logging:** All data access and privacy setting changes logged for compliance and governance purposes

### Scalability
- **Horizontal scaling:** All backend services deployed as stateless containers on AWS ECS Fargate, scaling independently based on load
- **Kafka partitioning:** Event streams partitioned by developer ID to ensure ordered processing per-developer while enabling parallel throughput across the fleet
- **TimescaleDB hypertables:** Automatic time-based partitioning and chunk compression for efficient storage and query performance as metric history grows
- **Multi-tenant isolation:** Organizational data isolated at the database schema level; team-scoped RBAC enforced at every API boundary
- **On-premise parity:** Kubernetes Helm chart deployment mirrors cloud architecture using the same Docker images, ensuring consistent scaling behavior in both deployment modes

### Compatibility
- **IDE platforms:** macOS, Windows, and Linux environments where Cursor IDE is installed
- **Cursor integration:** VS Code Extension API (`onDidChangeTextDocument`, language model API events, custom telemetry hooks) — compliant with Cursor's terms of service
- **Third-party integrations (Phase 2):** GitHub, GitLab, Jira, Linear via official APIs with OAuth authentication
- **Browser support:** Modern evergreen browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge)

### Infrastructure Cost Estimate
| Tier | Monthly Cost |
|---|---|
| Development / Staging | $800–$1,200 |
| Production (cloud) | $3,200–$6,800 |
| On-Premise (customer-hosted) | Infrastructure cost borne by customer; Helm chart licensing TBD |

---

## Success Metrics & KPIs

| Metric | Target | Measurement Method |
|---|---|---|
| AI suggestion acceptance rate improvement | +15% per developer within 90 days of platform use | TimescaleDB continuous aggregate, compared against 30-day baseline at onboarding |
| ROI report generation time | Under 5 minutes without manual data collection | Report Service job duration telemetry; user session timing in dashboard |
| Platform agent uptime | 99.5% uptime with <1% IDE performance degradation | AWS CloudWatch uptime monitoring; extension CPU profiling in beta program |
| Team activation rate | 80% of licensed developers active within 30 days of deployment | Daily active user count vs licensed seat count per organization |
| Dashboard load performance | Under 10 seconds for reports across up to 100 developers | GraphQL query latency P95 via Apollo Studio tracing |

key_decisions.title: Event-Driven Architecture with Kafka
key_decisions.choice: Apache Kafka as the central event bus with Apache Flink for stream processing
key_decisions.rationale: Cursor IDE generates extremely high-frequency telemetry events (keystrokes, autocomplete triggers, acceptances). Kafka decouples the IDE extension from backend processing, enables durable replay, handles burst traffic gracefully, and allows multiple consumers (stream processor, raw archiver, analytics) without data loss. Flink provides sub-second aggregations needed for the 10-second dashboard load requirement.

key_decisions.title: TimescaleDB for Metrics Storage
key_decisions.choice: TimescaleDB as the primary time-series store alongside PostgreSQL for relational data
key_decisions.rationale: Developer productivity metrics are inherently time-series data (events per hour, daily acceptance rates, session durations). TimescaleDB provides automatic partitioning, continuous aggregates, and compression that are 10-100x more efficient than vanilla PostgreSQL for this pattern, while still supporting standard SQL and JOINs against the relational analytics database.

key_decisions.title: GraphQL API Layer
key_decisions.choice: Apollo Server with GraphQL over REST
key_decisions.rationale: The dashboard has three very different consumer personas (Developer, Manager, DevOps) each needing distinct data shapes from the same underlying services. GraphQL eliminates over-fetching, allows the frontend to compose exactly the metrics it needs per view, and makes adding new metric fields non-breaking. Subscriptions also enable real-time dashboard updates without polling.

key_decisions.title: Cursor IDE Extension Strategy
key_decisions.choice: VS Code Extension API (since Cursor is VS Code-based) with a local telemetry agent
key_decisions.rationale: Cursor IDE is built on VS Code, meaning the full VS Code Extension API is available. The extension hooks into onDidChangeTextDocument, language model API events, and custom telemetry hooks. A local batching agent buffers events every 5 seconds to meet the <1% CPU constraint, sending compressed payloads rather than individual events, ensuring minimal performance impact.

key_decisions.title: Hybrid Deployment Architecture
key_decisions.choice: Cloud-native on AWS with a Kubernetes-based on-premise bundle
key_decisions.rationale: Enterprise customers with strict data residency requirements cannot send IDE telemetry to cloud. A containerized on-premise deployment using Kubernetes Helm charts mirrors the cloud architecture locally. Cloud customers use AWS ECS Fargate for managed scaling. The same Docker images are used in both, ensuring feature parity and simplified maintenance.

key_decisions.title: Privacy-First Data Model
key_decisions.choice: Opt-in telemetry with pseudonymization at ingestion and RBAC-enforced aggregation floors
key_decisions.rationale: Developer trust is critical for adoption. Raw events are pseudonymized with rotating developer IDs at the ingestion layer before hitting any database. Team views enforce a minimum cohort size (e.g., 5+ developers) before showing breakdowns to prevent manager surveillance of individuals. All controls live in a dedicated Privacy Service, ensuring governance is a first-class architectural concern, not an afterthought.

key_decisions.title: Claude API for Prompt Analysis
key_decisions.choice: Anthropic Claude API for the Prompt Effectiveness Analyzer feature
key_decisions.rationale: Analyzing which prompt patterns lead to accepted vs rejected Cursor AI suggestions requires nuanced understanding of developer intent and code context. Claude's large context window and code comprehension capabilities make it well-suited to identify prompt anti-patterns and generate actionable, personalized recommendations without storing raw code content.

key_decisions.title: Report Generation with PDF/CSV Export
key_decisions.choice: Server-side report rendering using Puppeteer (PDF) and fast-csv (CSV) within a dedicated Report Service
key_decisions.rationale: Engineering managers need polished, exportable reports for stakeholder presentations within 5 minutes. A dedicated Report Service pre-computes weekly/monthly aggregations on a schedule using TimescaleDB continuous aggregates, so report generation is a fast render operation rather than a live query, meeting the sub-5-minute requirement reliably.

# Work Orders

**Total Story Points:** 186 | **Sprints:** 8 | **Stories:** 18

---

## Epic 1: Cursor IDE Extension & Telemetry Pipeline
> Build the lightweight VS Code-based Cursor IDE extension and the high-throughput event ingestion pipeline that captures, batches, and streams developer activity data into the platform.

### [E1-S1] Cursor IDE Extension — Core Event Capture
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** extension, typescript, vscode-api, telemetry

**User Story**
As a developer, I want a background extension installed in Cursor IDE so that my AI-assisted coding activity is automatically captured without disrupting my workflow.

**Work Description**
Build a VS Code extension (TypeScript) that hooks into `onDidChangeTextDocument`, VS Code language model API events, and Cursor-specific telemetry hooks to capture autocomplete acceptances, chat interactions, code generation requests, and manual edit events. Implement a local in-memory buffer that batches events every 5 seconds into compressed payloads before dispatching to the API Gateway, ensuring the CPU overhead stays under 1%.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Extension captures the following event types with accurate timestamps: `autocomplete_accepted`, `autocomplete_rejected`, `chat_message_sent`, `composer_invoked`, `edit_mode_triggered`, `manual_keystroke_batch`
- [ ] Local batching agent accumulates events for a configurable interval (default 5s) and emits a single compressed (gzip) JSON payload per flush cycle
- [ ] Verified via CPU profiling that extension consumes < 1% CPU during a 60-minute active coding session on macOS, Windows, and Linux
- [ ] Extension gracefully queues events locally (up to 500 events) when the API Gateway is unreachable and retries with exponential backoff
- [ ] Unit tests cover event capture hooks, batching logic, and compression with ≥ 85% code coverage
- [ ] Extension is packaged as a `.vsix` and installable in a local Cursor IDE instance without errors

---

### [E1-S2] API Gateway & Auth Service
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, nestjs, auth, rbac, aws

**User Story**
As a platform operator, I want an authenticated API Gateway that validates all incoming telemetry from IDE extensions so that only authorized agents can write data into the system.

**Work Description**
Implement an NGINX-fronted NestJS API Gateway on AWS ECS Fargate that validates JWT tokens on inbound telemetry POST requests. Wire a dedicated Auth & RBAC Service that issues tokens to registered extension instances (per developer, per organization), enforces role assignments (Developer, Manager, DevOps, Admin), and returns 401/403 on unauthorized access. Configure AWS KMS for token signing key management.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] API Gateway exposes a `POST /v1/events` endpoint that accepts gzip-compressed JSON batches up to 1MB and returns `202 Accepted` on success
- [ ] JWT tokens are issued per developer identity, signed with AWS KMS-managed keys, and expire after 24 hours with a silent refresh flow in the extension
- [ ] RBAC roles (Developer, Manager, DevOps, Admin) are enforced at the gateway; requests with insufficient role return `403 Forbidden` with a structured error body
- [ ] Auth Service stores role assignments in PostgreSQL with an audit log table capturing all role changes with actor, timestamp, and previous value
- [ ] Gateway handles 1,000 concurrent connections without dropping requests, verified by a k6 load test
- [ ] All endpoints enforce HTTPS; plaintext HTTP connections are rejected with `301 Redirect`

---

### [E1-S3] Kafka Event Bus & Raw Event Archival
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, kafka, aws-msk, s3, infrastructure

**User Story**
As a platform engineer, I want all IDE telemetry events published to a durable Kafka bus so that multiple downstream consumers can process events independently without data loss.

**Work Description**
Provision AWS MSK (Managed Kafka) with two topics: `ide.events.raw` (retention 7 days, 12 partitions) and `ide.events.dlq` (dead letter queue). The NestJS Event Ingestion Service consumes validated payloads from the API Gateway and publishes individual events to `ide.events.raw`. A parallel S3 sink consumer subscribes to the same topic and writes gzip-compressed NDJSON files to an S3 raw event archive bucket organized by `org_id/year/month/day/hour/`.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Event Ingestion Service publishes each event from a batch payload as an individual Kafka message with a schema-validated Avro envelope containing `event_id`, `developer_id` (pseudonymized), `org_id`, `event_type`, `timestamp_utc`, and `payload`
- [ ] S3 sink consumer writes files to the archive bucket within 60 seconds of event arrival with partition key `org_id/YYYY/MM/DD/HH/`
- [ ] Failed Kafka publishes are routed to `ide.events.dlq` with original payload and failure reason; DLQ messages trigger a CloudWatch alarm after 100 messages accumulate
- [ ] Kafka topic throughput sustains 50,000 events/second in a load test without consumer lag exceeding 5 seconds
- [ ] Terraform modules provision MSK cluster, S3 bucket (with server-side encryption via KMS), and IAM roles; all infrastructure is version-controlled
- [ ] Integration test validates end-to-end flow: extension payload → API Gateway → Kafka topic → S3 file written within SLA

---

### [E1-S4] Flink Stream Processor — Real-Time Metric Aggregation
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** backend, flink, timescaledb, stream-processing

**User Story**
As a platform engineer, I want a stream processor that continuously aggregates raw IDE events into pre-computed metrics so that dashboards can load within 10 seconds even for teams of 100 developers.

**Work Description**
Deploy an Apache Flink job (running on ECS Fargate) that consumes from `ide.events.raw` and computes windowed aggregations: 1-minute tumbling windows for session-level metrics (acceptance rate, keystrokes, AI invocations) and 1-hour sliding windows for trend data. Write computed metrics to TimescaleDB hypertables and update Redis cache keys for frequently-queried developer and team aggregates. Define TimescaleDB continuous aggregates for daily and weekly rollups.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Flink job processes events from `ide.events.raw` with end-to-end latency (event creation to TimescaleDB write) under 3 seconds at p99
- [ ] TimescaleDB schema includes hypertables: `developer_session_metrics` (partitioned by hour), `autocomplete_events` (partitioned by day), `ai_feature_usage` (partitioned by day) — all with compression policies set to 7 days
- [ ] The following metrics are computed per developer per 1-minute window: `lines_ai_generated`, `lines_manually_typed`, `autocomplete_acceptance_rate`, `chat_interactions_count`, `session_active_seconds`
- [ ] Redis cache is populated with pre-aggregated daily summaries per `developer_id` and `org_id` using a `metrics:daily:{org_id}:{developer_id}:{date}` key pattern with 25-hour TTL
- [ ] TimescaleDB continuous aggregates for `weekly_developer_summary` and `monthly_team_summary` are defined and refresh on a 1-hour schedule
- [ ] Flink job recovers from checkpoint within 30 seconds of a simulated node failure without data duplication

---

## Epic 2: Metrics Dashboard & Analytics Views
> Deliver the Next.js React dashboard with role-specific views for developers, engineering managers, and DevOps engineers surfacing real-time and historical productivity metrics.

### [E2-S1] GraphQL API Layer & Data Services
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 3 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, graphql, apollo-server, nestjs, redis

**User Story**
As a frontend developer, I want a well-structured GraphQL API that exposes all metrics data so that I can compose exactly the fields needed for each dashboard view without over-fetching.

**Work Description**
Implement an Apollo Server 4 GraphQL API in NestJS that wraps the Metrics & Aggregation Service. Define schemas for `DeveloperMetrics`, `TeamMetrics`, `AiFeatureUsage`, and `SessionSummary` types. Implement resolvers that first check Redis cache, then fall back to TimescaleDB/PostgreSQL queries. Enable GraphQL subscriptions (WebSocket) for real-time metric updates pushed to active dashboard sessions.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] GraphQL schema defines query types: `developerDashboard(developerId, dateRange)`, `teamDashboard(orgId, dateRange)`, `aiFeatureHeatmap(developerId|orgId, dateRange)`, `productivityTrends(developerId|orgId, granularity)` — all documented in schema with descriptions
- [ ] Resolver cache-hit rate for daily summary queries exceeds 90% under normal load, measured via Apollo Server tracing metrics
- [ ] GraphQL subscription `onMetricsUpdated(developerId)` pushes updated 1-minute window metrics to subscribed clients within 5 seconds of Flink writing to TimescaleDB
- [ ] RBAC is enforced at the resolver level: a Manager can query `teamDashboard` only for their own `org_id`; Developers can only query their own `developerDashboard`
- [ ] GraphQL API returns p95 query response time under 500ms for dashboard queries against a dataset of 100 developers and 90 days of data
- [ ] Schema introspection is disabled in production; a schema registry document is published to the repo on every schema change via CI

---

### [E2-S2] Developer Personal Dashboard
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 3 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** frontend, nextjs, react, recharts, tailwind

**User Story**
As a developer, I want a personal productivity dashboard so that I can see my AI assistance metrics, coding trends, and feature utilization patterns to understand where I'm improving.

**Work Description**
Build the Developer View in Next.js 14 (App Router) using shadcn/ui components, Recharts for time-series charts, and TanStack Query for GraphQL data fetching. Render four primary widgets: (1) daily/weekly productivity trend line chart, (2) AI acceptance rate gauge with 30-day rolling average, (3) lines of AI-generated vs manually typed code stacked bar chart, and (4) AI feature usage frequency breakdown. Subscribe to real-time metric updates via GraphQL subscription.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Dashboard loads and renders all four widgets with live data within 3 seconds on a standard broadband connection (tested with Lighthouse)
- [ ] Productivity trend chart supports date range picker with presets (Today, Last 7 Days, Last 30 Days, Custom) and re-fetches data without full page reload
- [ ] AI acceptance rate gauge displays current rate, 30-day rolling average, and a color-coded indicator (red < 40%, yellow 40–65%, green > 65%)
- [ ] All charts are fully responsive and render correctly at 375px (mobile), 768px (tablet), and 1440px (desktop) breakpoints
- [ ] Empty state illustrations and copy are shown when a developer has fewer than 3 days of data
- [ ] Lighthouse performance score ≥ 85 on the developer dashboard page in production build

---

### [E2-S3] Engineering Manager Team Analytics View
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 4 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** frontend, nextjs, react, d3, rbac

**User Story**
As an engineering manager, I want a team-wide analytics view so that I can compare adoption rates across my team, identify developers who may need coaching, and track collective productivity trends.

**Work Description**
Build the Manager View in Next.js using D3.js for the team benchmarking scatter plot and Recharts for trend comparisons. Render: (1) team adoption rate scorecard (% of developers active in last 7 days), (2) developer benchmarking scatter plot (acceptance rate vs lines generated, each dot = one developer), (3) team productivity trend lines (aggregate), and (4) a developer ranking table with sortable columns. Enforce the privacy cohort floor: suppress individual developer breakdowns when team size is fewer than 5 active developers and display a privacy notice.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Team adoption rate scorecard updates daily and shows trend arrow (up/down/flat) compared to the previous 7-day period
- [ ] Benchmarking scatter plot renders one data point per developer with hover tooltips showing anonymized rank, acceptance rate, and AI usage score; clicking a developer point navigates to their detail view only if the manager has explicit `view_individual` permission
- [ ] Privacy cohort floor is enforced in the UI: when fewer than 5 developers have data for a selected period, individual breakdowns are hidden and a notice "Insufficient data to display individual metrics — minimum 5 active developers required" is shown
- [ ] Manager can filter all views by team tag, date range, and a minimum activity threshold (e.g., "only show developers with > 10 active sessions")
- [ ] Developer ranking table supports sorting by: AI acceptance rate, total AI-generated lines, session count, and last active date — with CSV export of the current view
- [ ] Role guard redirects non-Manager users attempting to access `/dashboard/team` to their personal dashboard with a toast notification

---

### [E2-S4] AI Feature Utilization Heatmap
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 4 | **Points:** 5
**Tags:** frontend, d3, backend, graphql

**User Story**
As a developer or engineering manager, I want a visual heatmap of which Cursor AI features are being used across sessions so that I can identify underutilized capabilities and prioritize coaching or self-improvement.

**Work Description**
Build a D3.js-powered heatmap component (used in both Developer and Manager views) where the X-axis represents days, the Y-axis represents AI feature categories (Autocomplete, Composer, Chat, Edit Mode), and cell color intensity encodes usage frequency. Wire to the `aiFeatureHeatmap` GraphQL query. Overlay an "effectiveness score" badge per feature row calculated from acceptance rate within each feature type. Provide an expandable detail drawer per feature row showing usage trend and a plaintext recommendation.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Heatmap renders with correct data bindings: each cell accurately reflects event counts for that feature × day combination from the GraphQL response
- [ ] Color scale is perceptually uniform (D3 `interpolateYlOrRd`) with a legend showing the min/max event count range for the current date selection
- [ ] Clicking any cell opens a detail tooltip showing: exact event count, acceptance rate for that feature on that day, and delta vs the developer's 30-day average for that feature
- [ ] Effectiveness score badge per feature row is calculated as `(accepted_suggestions / total_suggestions) * 100` and color-coded (same thresholds as acceptance rate gauge)
- [ ] Feature rows with effectiveness score < 40% display an inline recommendation chip (e.g., "Try Composer for multi-file refactors") sourced from a static recommendation map in the backend
- [ ] Component is exported as a reusable React component usable in both Developer and Manager views with a `scope` prop accepting `"individual"` or `"team"`

---

## Epic 3: Reports, Goals, Integrations & Prompt Analysis
> Deliver automated productivity reports, goal tracking, third-party toolchain integrations, and the Claude-powered prompt effectiveness analyzer.

### [E3-S1] Automated Productivity Report Generation
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 5 | **Points:** 8

As an engineering manager, I want automated weekly and monthly productivity reports exported as PDF and CSV so that I can share quantified ROI data with stakeholders without manual data collection.

Build a NestJS Report Generation Service that runs on a cron schedule (weekly Mondays 06:00 UTC, monthly 1st 06:00 UTC) and queries pre-computed TimescaleDB continuous aggregates to assemble report data. Use Puppeteer to render a styled HTML report template to PDF and `fast-csv` for CSV exports. Store generated reports in S3 with pre-signed download URLs. Trigger the Notification Service to email download links to subscribed managers.

# DORA Metrics Intelligence Platform — Product Requirements Document

---

> **Document Status:** Ready for Stakeholder Review
> **Prepared by:** Press — Forge Platform Documentation Agent
> **Version:** 1.0
> **Complexity:** High
> **Estimated Delivery:** 16 Weeks

---

## Executive Summary

Engineering organizations today operate in an environment where software delivery speed and reliability are primary competitive differentiators. Yet despite the proliferation of DevOps tooling, most engineering leaders remain unable to answer fundamental questions — *How often are we deploying? How long does it take to recover from an incident? Are we getting faster or slower?* — without spending hours manually assembling data from disconnected systems. The DORA Metrics Intelligence Platform solves this decisively by providing a single, automated, real-time command center for software delivery performance.

Built on the four research-validated metrics established by the DORA State of DevOps program — Deployment Frequency, Lead Time for Changes, Change Failure Rate, and Mean Time to Recovery — the platform transforms raw signals from an organization's existing toolchain into actionable intelligence. It natively integrates with the tools engineering teams already use: GitHub, GitLab, Jenkins, CircleCI, PagerDuty, Jira, and more. Within 30 minutes of initial setup, teams move from zero visibility to a fully populated dashboard benchmarked against industry standards, with no manual data wrangling required.

The platform is architected for the enterprise from day one. A microservices-based design on AWS EKS, a Kafka-powered event ingestion backbone, and TimescaleDB for time-series metric storage ensure the system remains performant and reliable at scale — handling over one million deployment events per tenant while maintaining sub-5-minute data freshness. With SOC 2 Type II compliance, SAML 2.0/OIDC SSO, and row-level multi-tenant data isolation, the platform meets the security and compliance bar required by engineering organizations in regulated industries. The result is a platform that doesn't just report on engineering performance — it actively accelerates it.

---

## The Problem

Engineering leaders at scaling organizations face a systemic visibility gap. The tools that power modern software delivery — version control systems, CI/CD pipelines, incident management platforms, and project trackers — each capture fragments of the delivery story, but none speak to each other. The result is that when a VP of Engineering needs to answer "Why did our deployment frequency drop last quarter?" or "Which team has the worst change failure rate?", the answer requires hours of manual data extraction from GitHub, Jenkins, and PagerDuty, followed by spreadsheet gymnastics that are error-prone, stale by the time they're shared, and impossible to reproduce consistently.

This fragmentation creates three compounding problems. First, **blind spots masquerade as normalcy** — without objective, continuous measurement, teams don't know they have a deployment bottleneck until a major incident reveals it. Second, **improvement efforts lack feedback loops** — DevOps initiatives and process changes are adopted on faith, with no reliable mechanism to measure whether lead times actually improved or change failure rates actually decreased. Third, **reporting becomes a tax on engineering productivity** — engineering managers spend an estimated 4–6 hours per week assembling status reports for stakeholders, time that should be invested in unblocking teams and improving systems.

The cost compounds as organizations scale. At 10 engineers, informal awareness compensates for missing metrics. At 100 or 1,000 engineers across multiple teams and microservices, the absence of unified delivery performance data means that high-performing teams cannot be identified and celebrated, struggling teams cannot be identified and supported, and the entire organization operates on gut feel in an environment that demands data-driven decision-making.

---

## The Solution

The DORA Metrics Intelligence Platform eliminates the visibility gap by acting as a persistent, automated data aggregation and intelligence layer that sits across an organization's entire DevOps toolchain. It ingests events from every stage of the software delivery lifecycle — a commit pushed to GitHub, a CI build completed in CircleCI, a deployment triggered in Jenkins, an incident fired in PagerDuty — normalizes them into a unified event model, and continuously computes the four DORA metrics in near real time.

What separates this platform from generic dashboard tools is its domain specificity. Every feature — from the deployment pipeline timeline that breaks lead time into granular stages, to the incident tracker that automatically links production failures back to the deployments that caused them, to the AI-assisted recommendations engine that surfaces actionable improvement opportunities — is designed around the specific workflows and questions of engineering organizations. Teams don't configure a generic BI tool; they connect their existing tools and immediately receive meaningful, contextualized intelligence.

The platform's value compounds over time. In week one, teams gain immediate visibility into where they currently stand. By week four, trend data reveals whether changes to deployment processes are having measurable impact. By month three, benchmark comparisons and goal-setting workflows transform DORA metrics from passive measurements into active improvement targets embedded in team OKRs. Engineering leaders stop reporting on the past and start managing toward a measurable future.

---

## Technical Requirements

### Architecture Overview

The platform is built on a **microservices architecture** deployed on **AWS EKS (Kubernetes)**, decomposed into 8+ independently deployable services communicating via a **Kafka event bus**. This design enables the webhook ingestion layer to scale independently from the reporting and recommendations services, which have fundamentally different throughput and latency profiles.

```
Clients (React SPA / PWA / Stakeholder View)
    ↓
API Gateway (Kong) + Auth Service (SAML / OIDC)
    ↓
Core Services: Metrics Engine · Pipeline Service · Incident Service
               Segment Service · Alert Service · Report Service
               Recommendations Engine · Benchmark Service
    ↑
Event Bus (Kafka → Flink Stream Processor → Normalization Layer)
    ↑
Ingestion Layer: Webhook Gateway · Integration Hub · Public REST API
    ↑
External Tools: GitHub · GitLab · Jenkins · CircleCI · PagerDuty · Jira
```

---

### Performance

- **Data Freshness:** All DORA metrics must be calculated and reflected in the dashboard within **≤5 minutes** of a real-world deployment or incident event occurring in any integrated tool
- **Dashboard Responsiveness:** Core dashboard views must load within **≤2 seconds** for datasets up to 1 million deployment events per tenant, enforced via Redis caching of pre-aggregated metric windows and TimescaleDB continuous aggregates
- **Ingestion Throughput:** The Webhook Ingestion Gateway (Go-based) must sustain **≥10,000 webhook events per second** per tenant to accommodate high-frequency monorepo commit patterns without back-pressure
- **Kafka Event Bus:** AWS MSK provides durable, replayable event ingestion with Apache Flink handling stateful stream processing at sub-5-minute recalculation windows with exactly-once semantics
- **Search & Indexing:** Elasticsearch 8 (AWS OpenSearch) indexes all deployment and incident events for full-text search, anomaly queries, and the recommendations engine's pattern analysis

### Security & Compliance

- **SOC 2 Type II:** Full compliance required. Audit logging, encryption at rest (AWS KMS), encryption in transit (TLS 1.3), and access control audit trails are mandatory across all services
- **Authentication:** OAuth 2.0 for all third-party integrations; SAML 2.0 and OIDC for enterprise SSO; API key authentication for REST API and webhook endpoints
- **Multi-Tenancy Isolation:** PostgreSQL Row-Level Security (RLS) policies enforce tenant data isolation at the database layer, independent of application logic, as a compliance backstop. All queries include mandatory `tenant_id` predicates enforced via RLS
- **Webhook Security:** All inbound webhook payloads are verified via HMAC signature validation before acceptance into the event pipeline
- **Secrets Management:** All integration credentials, OAuth tokens, and API keys stored encrypted via AWS KMS. No credentials stored in application code or environment variables
