## Epic 1: Foundation — Auth, Infrastructure & Multi-Tenant Data Layer
> Establish the secure, multi-tenant infrastructure backbone including authentication, database schema, and Kubernetes deployment scaffolding that all other services depend on.

### [E1-S1] Multi-Tenant PostgreSQL Schema & Row-Level Security
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, database, security

**User Story**
As a platform engineer, I want tenant data isolated at the database layer so that no cross-tenant data leakage is possible even if application logic has a bug.

**Work Description**
Design and implement the PostgreSQL 15 schema with a `tenants` table as the root entity. Apply Row-Level Security (RLS) policies on all core tables (`teams`, `repositories`, `deployments`, `incidents`, `metrics`) enforcing `tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant')`. Implement a `set_tenant_context()` helper called at the start of every database session from the connection pool. Create TimescaleDB hypertables for `deployment_events` and `metric_snapshots` partitioned by `(tenant_id, time)` with 7-day chunk intervals and continuous aggregate policies for hourly and daily rollups.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] RLS policies are active on all tables; a query executed without setting tenant context returns zero rows and does not error silently
- [ ] TimescaleDB hypertables for `deployment_events` and `metric_snapshots` are created with verified chunk partitioning by `tenant_id + time`
- [ ] Continuous aggregate materialized views exist for 1-hour and 1-day DORA metric rollups and refresh within 5 minutes of new data insertion
- [ ] A unit test suite proves cross-tenant data isolation: tenant A credentials cannot retrieve tenant B rows under any query path
- [ ] Database migration scripts are idempotent and managed via Flyway with version history tracked in `schema_migrations`

---

### [E1-S2] Auth Service — SSO, SAML 2.0, OIDC & API Key Management
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, security, auth

**User Story**
As an enterprise engineering manager, I want to log in via my company's SSO provider so that my team doesn't need separate credentials and access is governed by our existing identity policies.

**Work Description**
Build a Node.js Auth Service using Passport.js with strategies for SAML 2.0 (via `passport-saml`) and OIDC (via `openid-client`). Implement standard email/password login as fallback with bcrypt hashing. Issue short-lived JWTs (15-minute expiry) with refresh token rotation stored in Redis with `tenant_id` and `role` claims. Build an API key management module supporting creation, rotation, and revocation of SHA-256-hashed API keys scoped to a tenant, stored in PostgreSQL. Expose endpoints: `POST /auth/saml`, `POST /auth/oidc/callback`, `POST /auth/token/refresh`, `POST /auth/api-keys`, `DELETE /auth/api-keys/:id`.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] SAML 2.0 login flow completes end-to-end with Okta as the test IdP, with `tenant_id` resolved from `organizationId` attribute mapping
- [ ] OIDC login flow completes end-to-end with Google Workspace as the test provider
- [ ] JWT access tokens expire in 15 minutes; refresh tokens rotate on use and are invalidated in Redis on logout
- [ ] API keys are stored as SHA-256 hashes only; plaintext is returned once at creation time and never again
- [ ] All auth endpoints return RFC 7807 Problem Details error responses; brute-force protection locks an account after 5 failed attempts within 10 minutes

---

### [E1-S3] Kong API Gateway Configuration & Rate Limiting
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 5
**Tags:** backend, infrastructure, security

**User Story**
As a platform engineer, I want a centralized API gateway enforcing auth and rate limits so that individual services don't need to reimplement these cross-cutting concerns.

**Work Description**
Deploy Kong Gateway on EKS with declarative configuration (`deck` YAML). Configure upstream routes for all eight core services with JWT validation plugin pointing to the Auth Service's JWKS endpoint. Implement rate limiting plugin using Redis backend: 1,000 requests/minute per `tenant_id` for standard tiers, 10,000/minute for enterprise tiers stored as tenant metadata. Configure the correlation-id plugin to propagate `X-Request-ID` headers through all upstream calls for distributed tracing. Set up Kong's health check plugin against each upstream service's `/health` endpoint.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] All requests to `/api/*` without a valid JWT receive a `401 Unauthorized` response; requests with an expired JWT receive a distinct `401` with `"error": "token_expired"`
- [ ] Rate limit headers (`X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`) are returned on every API response
- [ ] A request exceeding the tenant rate limit returns `429 Too Many Requests` with a `Retry-After` header
- [ ] `X-Request-ID` is present and consistent across all upstream service logs for a single request, verified via Datadog trace search
- [ ] Kong config is fully declarative in version-controlled YAML and can be applied idempotently via `deck sync` in CI

### [E1-S4] Kubernetes EKS Cluster, Terraform IaC & CI/CD Pipeline
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** infrastructure, devops

**User Story**
As a platform engineer, I want the entire infrastructure defined as code so that environments are reproducible, auditable, and deployable in under 30 minutes.

**Work Description**
Write Terraform modules for AWS EKS cluster (1.29), AWS MSK Kafka cluster (3-broker, `kafka.m5.xlarge`), AWS RDS PostgreSQL 15 with TimescaleDB extension enabled, AWS ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster (3-node), AWS OpenSearch 2.x domain, and AWS S3 buckets with server-side encryption via KMS. Create Kubernetes namespace-per-service with resource quotas and network policies blocking cross-namespace traffic except through the API gateway. Implement GitHub Actions workflows for: lint/test on PR, build and push Docker images to ECR on merge to `main`, and `helm upgrade --install` per service on tag push.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] `terraform apply` from a clean AWS account provisions all infrastructure with zero manual steps; estimated runtime under 25 minutes
- [ ] All S3 buckets, RDS instances, and MSK topics have KMS encryption-at-rest enabled; verified via `terraform plan` showing no unencrypted resources
- [ ] Kubernetes network policies are verified: a pod in the `metrics-engine` namespace cannot make a direct TCP connection to a pod in the `incident-service` namespace
- [ ] GitHub Actions CI pipeline completes lint + unit tests in under 8 minutes and Docker build + ECR push in under 6 minutes on a standard runner
- [ ] Each service has a `Dockerfile` producing an image under 200MB using multi-stage builds, with no `root` user at runtime

## Epic 2: Data Ingestion — Webhook Gateway, Integration Hub & Event Normalization
> Build the high-throughput data ingestion layer that receives events from all external DevOps tools, normalizes them into a canonical schema, and publishes to Kafka for downstream processing.

### [E2-S1] Go Webhook Ingestion Gateway
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, go, ingestion

**User Story**
As a DevOps engineer, I want webhook events from GitHub, Jenkins, and PagerDuty to be ingested reliably at high volume so that deployment and incident data flows into the dashboard within minutes of real-world events.

**Work Description**
Build a Go service using `net/http` with goroutine-per-connection handling targeting 10,000 concurrent connections. Implement HMAC-SHA256 signature verification for each supported source (GitHub uses `X-Hub-Signature-256`, PagerDuty uses its own header scheme). Parse and fan out events by source type to typed structs. Publish verified events to Kafka topic `raw-events` with `tenant_id` as the partition key (ensuring ordered processing per tenant). Implement an in-memory circuit breaker using `gobreaker` that stops Kafka writes and returns `503` if the broker is unreachable for more than 10 seconds, with dead-letter queuing to Redis for replay.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Gateway rejects webhooks with invalid HMAC signatures with `401 Unauthorized`; valid events are acknowledged with `200 OK` within 200ms at p99 under 5,000 concurrent requests (load tested via k6)
- [ ] Events from GitHub `push`, `pull_request`, and `deployment_status` webhooks are correctly parsed and published to Kafka with `tenant_id` partition key
- [ ] Dead-letter queue in Redis stores rejected/failed events with source metadata; a `POST /webhooks/replay/:event_id` endpoint re-publishes a DLQ event to Kafka
- [ ] Circuit breaker opens after 10 seconds of Kafka broker unavailability and returns `503`; it half-opens after 30 seconds and closes on first successful write
- [ ] Service emits Prometheus metrics: `webhook_events_received_total`, `webhook_events_failed_total`, `kafka_publish_latency_histogram` — scraped by Datadog agent

---

### [E2-S2] Multi-Tool Integration Hub — OAuth Setup Wizard & Polling Connector
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, go, integrations, oauth

**User Story**
As an engineering manager, I want a guided OAuth setup wizard so that I can connect GitHub, PagerDuty, and Jira to the dashboard in under 30 minutes without involving my security team.

**Work Description**
Build a Go service implementing OAuth 2.0 authorization code flows for GitHub (repos + deployments scopes), GitLab, Bitbucket, Jenkins (API token), CircleCI, GitHub Actions (via GitHub App), PagerDuty, Opsgenie, Jira, and Linear. Store encrypted OAuth tokens in PostgreSQL using AES-256-GCM via AWS KMS. Implement per-integration polling workers using `time.Ticker` at configurable intervals (default 5 minutes) for sources that don't support webhooks (Jira, Linear). Publish polled events to the same `raw-events` Kafka topic. Expose `GET /integrations`, `POST /integrations/:provider/connect`, `DELETE /integrations/:provider` endpoints.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] OAuth flow for GitHub completes in under 5 clicks from the setup wizard UI; access token is stored encrypted and never returned in any API response after initial exchange
- [ ] Polling worker for Jira fetches issues updated since last poll watermark and publishes to Kafka without duplicates (idempotency key = `{tenant_id}:{provider}:{external_id}`)
- [ ] `GET /integrations` returns connection status, last successful sync timestamp, and event count per integration for the authenticated tenant
- [ ] Revoking an integration via `DELETE /integrations/:provider` deletes the stored token, stops the polling worker, and emits a `integration.disconnected` event to Kafka within 5 seconds
- [ ] Integration Hub handles OAuth token refresh automatically when a `401` is received from any provider; failed refreshes trigger a `integration.auth_expired` alert event

---

### [E2-S3] Flink Stream Processor & Data Normalization Layer
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, streaming, flink

**User Story**
As a platform engineer, I want all incoming events from disparate tools to be normalized into a canonical DORA event schema so that the metrics engine can process them uniformly regardless of source.

**Work Description**
Implement an Apache Flink job consuming from the `raw-events` Kafka topic. Define a canonical `DoraNormalizedEvent` schema with fields: `event_id`, `tenant_id`, `event_type` (enum: `DEPLOYMENT`, `INCIDENT_OPEN`, `INCIDENT_CLOSE`, `COMMIT`, `PR_MERGED`, `BUILD`), `service_id`, `environment`, `timestamp`, `source_tool`, `raw_payload_ref`. Write per-source transformation functions for each of the 10 integrated tools mapping native schemas to the canonical model. Publish normalized events to `normalized-events` Kafka topic. Implement a Flink keyed-state window (5-minute tumbling) for deduplication using `event_id`. Route to `normalized-deployments`, `normalized-incidents`, and `normalized-vcs` topic partitions.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] A GitHub `deployment_status` webhook event with status `success` is consumed from `raw-events` and a correctly typed `DEPLOYMENT` normalized event appears in `normalized-deployments` within 30 seconds
- [ ] Duplicate events with identical `event_id` within a 5-minute window are deduplicated; only one normalized event is emitted (verified with a replay test injecting 100 duplicate events)
- [ ] Normalization coverage: all 10 source tools have transformation functions with unit tests asserting correct field mapping for at least 3 representative event payloads each
- [ ] Flink job recovers from a simulated broker restart within 60 seconds and resumes from last committed Kafka offset with no event loss (exactly-once semantics via Kafka transaction API)
- [ ] Unknown/unsupported event types are routed to a `dead-letter-normalized` topic with the raw payload preserved for debugging

---

### [E2-S4] Public REST API & Webhook Ingestion Endpoint
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** medium | **Sprint:** 3 | **Points:** 5
**Tags:** backend, api

**User Story**
As a DevOps engineer with a proprietary CI/CD pipeline, I want to push deployment events via a REST API so that my custom tooling can contribute data to the DORA dashboard without requiring a native integration.

**Work Description**
Build a Node.js/Express public API service exposing `POST /ingest/deployments` and `POST /ingest/incidents` endpoints authenticated via API keys (validated against the Auth Service). Define and document JSON schemas for both event types using JSON Schema Draft-07 with OpenAPI 3.1 spec. Implement request validation using `ajv`; invalid payloads return structured `400` errors listing all validation failures. Publish valid events directly to the `raw-events` Kafka topic with `source_tool: "custom_api"`. Add an event replay endpoint `POST /ingest/replay` accepting an array of up to 1,000 historical events with a `replay: true` flag to prevent alert triggering. Generate and host interactive API docs via Swagger UI at `/api/docs`.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] `POST /ingest/deployments` with a valid payload returns `202 Accepted` with an `event_id` within 150ms p99
- [ ] A payload missing required fields (`service_id`, `environment`, `deployed_at`) returns `400 Bad Request` with a response body listing each missing field path using JSON Pointer notation
- [ ] API key authentication: a request with an invalid or revoked API key returns `401`; a request from a valid key increments the key's `last_used_at` timestamp in PostgreSQL
- [ ] Replay endpoint accepts a batch of 1,000 events and publishes all to Kafka within 10 seconds; replayed events do not trigger alert evaluations
- [ ] OpenAPI 3.1 spec is auto-generated from route definitions and accessible at `/api/docs/openapi.json`; spec passes `spectral lint` with zero errors

# CloudLift — Product Requirements Document

> **Prepared by:** Press, Documentation Agent — Forge Platform
> **Document Version:** 1.0
> **Status:** Ready for Stakeholder Review
> **Classification:** Internal — Confidential

---

## Executive Summary

Deploying applications to AWS remains one of the most persistent sources of friction in modern software development. Despite AWS being the world's leading cloud platform, its complexity — spanning hundreds of services, intricate IAM permission models, region-specific configurations, and service-specific deployment patterns — creates a steep barrier that costs engineering teams thousands of hours annually. CloudLift is a streamlined AWS deployment platform built to eliminate that friction entirely, giving developers a clear, intuitive path from code commit to live production deployment without requiring deep AWS expertise.

CloudLift abstracts away the operational complexity of AWS infrastructure while preserving the power and flexibility that makes AWS the platform of choice for serious engineering teams. By auto-detecting project types, generating optimized deployment pipelines, and managing credentials securely through IAM role assumption, CloudLift transforms what is typically a multi-hour, error-prone process into a sub-10-minute experience. Deployments to ECS, Lambda, Elastic Beanstalk, and EC2 become standardized, auditable, and repeatable — not tribal knowledge locked in a senior engineer's head.

At the organizational level, CloudLift delivers the visibility, governance, and control that Engineering Managers and DevOps teams require: centralized deployment dashboards, role-based access control, full audit trails, environment isolation, and AI-powered Infrastructure as Code generation that keeps teams out of the weeds and focused on building product. With intelligent cost estimation, one-click rollback, and deep CI/CD integration, CloudLift is not just a deployment tool — it is the operational backbone for any team serious about shipping software reliably on AWS.

---

## The Problem

Deploying code to AWS is fundamentally broken for the majority of engineering teams. The root issue is not that AWS lacks capability — it is that AWS was designed for maximum flexibility rather than developer experience, and that gap between raw power and practical usability costs organizations dearly. Setting up a working deployment pipeline for even a simple containerized application requires navigating IAM roles, ECR repositories, ECS cluster configuration, load balancer rules, security groups, VPC subnets, and CloudWatch log groups — each with its own configuration surface and failure modes. A misconfigured trust policy or missing permission boundary can block an entire team's deployment for hours.

The consequences are measurable and severe. Developers context-switch away from feature work to debug AWS configuration errors. Junior engineers cannot deploy independently without senior oversight, creating bottlenecks. Inconsistent deployment practices across teams lead to divergent infrastructure states that are expensive and risky to reconcile. Rollbacks — which should be a last resort executed in seconds — frequently take 15–30 minutes of manual intervention during high-stress incidents. Meanwhile, Engineering Managers have no centralized view of what is being deployed, by whom, to which environment, or at what cost. Misconfigured AWS resources quietly inflate cloud bills, and compliance teams cannot generate the audit trails required for SOC 2 or internal governance requirements without substantial custom tooling.

The market has partial answers — Terraform handles IaC, GitHub Actions handles CI/CD triggers, AWS CloudFormation handles resource provisioning — but none of these tools speak to each other coherently out of the box, and assembling them into a reliable deployment system requires significant platform engineering investment that most product teams cannot afford. CloudLift closes this gap by providing a unified, opinionated, and intelligent deployment platform that makes the right thing the easy thing for every engineer on the team.

---

## The Solution

CloudLift solves the AWS deployment problem through a combination of intelligent automation, secure-by-default architecture, and a unified operational interface that serves every role in the engineering organization simultaneously. Rather than wrapping AWS APIs in a thin UI, CloudLift implements a complete deployment abstraction layer — one that auto-detects project type from repository contents, generates production-grade deployment pipelines without manual configuration, and manages all AWS interactions through short-lived STS AssumeRole credentials that never touch CloudLift's own storage.

What makes CloudLift uniquely effective is the integration of AI-powered Infrastructure as Code generation using Claude Sonnet, which analyzes project metadata, detected dependencies, and target environment context to produce correct, optimized CloudFormation and Terraform templates. This eliminates the maintenance burden of static template libraries and enables CloudLift to support long-tail AWS service configurations that rule-based systems cannot handle. Paired with a BullMQ-based deployment orchestration engine that handles retries, partial failures, and AWS rate limits gracefully, and a real-time log streaming system built on Server-Sent Events and Redis Pub/Sub, CloudLift delivers the deployment reliability and observability that teams actually need in production.

Critically, CloudLift is designed to grow with teams rather than constrain them. Individual developers get one-click deployments with zero AWS knowledge required. DevOps engineers get standardized pipelines, environment isolation, and infrastructure-as-code exports they can customize. Engineering Managers get centralized dashboards, RBAC-enforced approval workflows, audit logs, and cost visibility. The platform hosts itself on Google Cloud Run to avoid circular dependency on AWS, sits behind Cloudflare for edge security, and uses HashiCorp Vault for secrets management — a production-grade architecture that demonstrates exactly the operational maturity CloudLift enables its customers to achieve.

---

## User Personas

### Alex Chen — Individual Developer

> *"I just want to ship my code. I shouldn't need to become an AWS expert to deploy a Node.js API."*

**Background:** Full-stack developer with 2–4 years of experience. Comfortable with Git, modern JavaScript frameworks, and basic cloud concepts, but lacks deep AWS knowledge. Works on small teams or solo on side projects.

**Goals:**
- Deploy applications quickly without spending hours reading AWS documentation
- Get clear, actionable feedback when deployments fail
- Manage environment variables and secrets without touching the AWS console
- Focus time on writing product features rather than configuring infrastructure

**Pain Points:**
- The AWS CLI and console are overwhelming; there are too many services and options with unclear defaults
- IAM permissions errors are cryptic and hard to debug without senior help
- No clear visibility into whether a deployment succeeded or what broke if it failed
- Setting up a staging environment that mirrors production requires duplicating a complex, manual process
- Accidentally exposing credentials or misconfiguring security groups is a constant anxiety

**How CloudLift Helps:**
CloudLift's one-click deployment pipeline auto-detects Alex's project type and generates an optimized pipeline without any AWS configuration required. The real-time log dashboard surfaces build output, deployment status, and health check results in a readable interface — no CloudWatch console navigation needed. Environment management lets Alex create isolated dev and staging environments with a single action, with secrets stored securely in Vault-backed environment variable management. AWS credential management is fully abstracted via IAM role assumption, so Alex never handles access keys directly.

---

### Jordan Rivera — DevOps / Platform Engineer

> *"I'm managing deployment pipelines for six teams and every one of them does it differently. One incident and I can't tell what's deployed where."*

**Background:** Senior DevOps or platform engineer responsible for CI/CD infrastructure, deployment standards, and production reliability across multiple engineering teams. Deep AWS knowledge but stretched thin maintaining bespoke pipelines for every project.

**Goals:**
- Standardize deployment practices across all teams without being a bottleneck
- Roll back failed deployments in under two minutes during production incidents
- Maintain environment-specific configurations at scale without configuration drift
- Integrate deployments cleanly into existing Git-based workflows

**Pain Points:**
- Every team has rolled their own deployment scripts; onboarding a new project means learning a new system
- Rolling back a failed production deployment requires manual intervention in the AWS console under pressure
- Environment variables and secrets are inconsistently managed — some in SSM, some hardcoded, some in `.env` files committed to Git
- Maintaining approval gates for production deployments across teams requires custom tooling

**How CloudLift Helps:**
CloudLift gives Jordan a single platform where all team deployments are standardized, auditable, and manageable. Rollback to any previous deployment version is a single click, with automated triggers available based on health check failures. Environment management enforces consistent secrets handling via AWS Secrets Manager integration across all projects. RBAC and approval workflows allow Jordan to enforce production deployment gates without building custom tooling. CI/CD integrations with GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket slot into existing workflows rather than replacing them.

---

### Morgan Wu — Engineering Manager

> *"I have no idea what my team shipped last Tuesday, whether it caused the spike in errors, or what it cost us. That's not acceptable."*

**Background:** Engineering Manager or VP of Engineering overseeing 2–4 teams. Responsible for delivery velocity, production reliability, cloud cost governance, and compliance with internal audit and security requirements.

**Goals:**
- Maintain clear visibility into what is being deployed across all teams and environments
- Reduce onboarding time for new engineers joining the team
- Control AWS cost overruns from misconfigured or over-provisioned resources
- Produce audit trails for production deployments to satisfy compliance requirements

**Pain Points:**
- No centralized view of deployment activity — must ask engineers directly or dig through CloudWatch logs
- New engineers take weeks to make their first independent AWS deployment, blocking productivity
- AWS bills are unpredictable; resource over-provisioning due to copy-pasted configurations is common
- SOC 2 and internal security reviews require deployment audit trails that don't currently exist in a usable form

**How CloudLift Helps:**
CloudLift's analytics dashboard gives Morgan a real-time view of deployment frequency, success rates, mean time to recovery, and trends across all projects and environments in one interface. New engineers make their first deployment in under 10 minutes by following CloudLift's guided flow, dramatically compressing onboarding time. The pre-deployment cost estimator surfaces projected AWS spend before resources are provisioned, with right-sizing recommendations powered by Claude Haiku. Every production deployment generates a complete, queryable audit log with actor, timestamp, diff, and outcome — ready for compliance review without additional tooling.

---

## Feature Requirements

### MVP — Phase 1

*Foundational deployment capability, core security model, and essential observability. Target completion: Weeks 1–12.*

| Feature | Description | Priority | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| **One-Click Deployment Pipeline** | Auto-detect project type (Node.js, Python, containerized apps) from repository content and generate optimized AWS deployment pipelines for ECS, Lambda, and Elastic Beanstalk with zero manual configuration | High | XL |
| **AWS Service Connector** | Cross-account IAM role integration using AWS STS AssumeRole for all AWS operations. Users create a trust-policy-linked IAM role in their account; platform assumes it on demand with short-lived credentials. No long-lived keys stored | High | XL |
| **Environment Management** | Create and manage dev, staging, and production environments with isolated configurations. Environment variables stored encrypted in HashiCorp Vault with per-environment access scoping and injection at deploy time via AWS Secrets Manager | High | L |
| **Real-Time Deployment Logs** | Live log streaming during deployments using Server-Sent Events with Redis Pub/Sub as the internal broadcast bus. CloudWatch integration surfaces post-deployment application logs. Rendered in-browser using Xterm.js for terminal-native readability | High | L |
| **Deployment Health Checks** | Post-deployment automated health checks with configurable endpoints, timeout thresholds, and failure alerting. Integrates with CloudWatch alarms for service-level monitoring | High | M |
| **Rollback & Version History** | One-click rollback to any previous deployment version with full deployment history log, change diffs, and automated rollback triggers on health check failure. Mean time to rollback target: under 2 minutes | High | L |
| **GitHub / GitLab Integration** | Webhook-based CI/CD triggers on push and pull request merge events. OAuth2 authentication via Passport.js. Repository browsing and branch selection in the deployment UI | High | M |
| **Auth & User Management** | JWT-based authentication with OAuth2 support (GitHub, GitLab). Basic role model (Owner, Member) with session management via Redis. Secure multi-tenant data isolation in PostgreSQL | High | M |
| **Project Dashboard** | Per-project deployment status overview showing current deployed version, environment health, recent deployment history, and quick-action buttons for deploy and rollback | High | M |

---

### Phase 2 — Governance, Intelligence & Scale

*Team collaboration features, AI-powered capabilities, cost management, and advanced analytics. Target completion: Weeks 13–20.*

| Feature | Description | Priority | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Team RBAC & Approval Workflows** | Role-based access control with Admin, DevOps, Developer, and Viewer roles. Production deployment approval gates requiring designated approver sign-off. Full audit log of all deployment actions with actor, timestamp, and outcome | Medium | L |
| **Infrastructure as Code Generator** | AI-powered CloudFormation and Terraform template generation using Claude Sonnet, based on detected project type, selected AWS service, and environment configuration. Templates are exportable and editable for advanced users | Medium | XL |
| **Cost Estimator & Resource Optimizer** | Pre-deployment cost projection using AWS Pricing API, surfaced before infrastructure is provisioned. Claude Haiku-powered right-sizing recommendations based on application type and expected traffic patterns | Medium | L |
| **Bitbucket Integration** | Extend CI/CD webhook support to Bitbucket repositories, completing the core Git provider triangle | Medium | S |
| **Deployment Analytics Dashboard** | Centralized metrics dashboard showing deployment frequency, success rate, mean time to recovery, and deployment trends over time. Powered by TimescaleDB hypertables and continuous aggregates with Recharts visualizations | Medium | M |
| **Multi-Account & Multi-Region Support** | Connect and manage deployments across multiple AWS accounts and regions from a single CloudLift workspace. Account-level isolation with cross-account role chaining | Medium | L |
| **EC2 Deployment Support** | Extend the deployment engine to support direct EC2 deployments with AMI management, user data scripts, and Auto Scaling Group integration | Low | L |

---

**Effort Scale:** XS < S < M < L < XL

---

## Technical Requirements

### Architecture Overview

CloudLift is built on a microservices architecture hosted on Google Cloud Run, with each service independently deployable and scalable. All inter-service communication uses tRPC for end-to-end type safety. The deployment orchestration layer uses BullMQ on Redis for asynchronous, fault-tolerant pipeline execution.

```
Developer Browser
      │
      ├─── Next.js Frontend (React + TypeScript)
      │         │
      │    Cloudflare (CDN / WAF / DDoS)
      │         │
      ├─── API Gateway (Nginx + Rate Limiting)
      │         │
      │    ┌────┴─────────────────────────────────┐
      │    │ Auth │ Projects │ Deploy │ Env │ Cost │
      │    └────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
      │         │
      │    BullMQ Job Queue (Redis)
      │         │
      │    Deployment Workers
      │         │
      │    AWS Connector (STS AssumeRole)
      │         │
      │    ┌────┴──────────────────────┐
      │    │ ECS │ Lambda │ EB │ CFn   │
      │    └───────────────────────────┘
      │
      └─── SSE Log Stream ← Redis Pub/Sub ← Workers
```

### Performance Requirements

| Requirement | Target |
|---|---|
| API response time (p95) | < 200ms for all non-deployment endpoints |
| Deployment pipeline initiation latency | < 3 seconds from trigger to first worker pickup |
| Real-time log streaming latency | < 500ms from CloudWatch event to browser render |
| Dashboard page load time | < 1.5 seconds (LCP) on standard connection |
| Concurrent deployments supported per tenant | Up to 10 simultaneous environments |
| WebSocket / SSE connection capacity | 10,000 concurrent streaming connections |
| Rollback execution time | < 2 minutes from trigger to traffic shift |

### Security Requirements

- **Credential Model:** All AWS operations performed via STS AssumeRole with short-lived tokens (maximum 1-hour TTL). No long-lived AWS access keys stored anywhere in the platform.
- **Secrets Encryption:** All environment variables and secrets encrypted at rest using HashiCorp Vault with AES-256 envelope encryption. Secrets are injected at deploy time and never logged.
- **Transport Security:** TLS 1.3 enforced on all external endpoints. Cloudflare WAF with OWASP ruleset active on all traffic ingress.
- **Authentication:** JWT tokens with 15-minute expiry. Refresh token rotation on every use. OAuth2 tokens stored encrypted, never in localStorage.
- **Tenant Isolation:** Strict PostgreSQL row-level security enforcing tenant boundary on all queries.

infrastructure: Google Cloud Run (platform hosting — avoids AWS circular dependency)
ai_ml: Claude API (project type auto-detection and IaC template generation)
Claude Sonnet (CloudFormation/Terraform template generation and optimization)

key_decisions.title: Host Platform on Google Cloud, Not AWS
key_decisions.choice: Google Cloud Run + Cloud SQL as the hosting infrastructure
key_decisions.rationale: Technical constraints explicitly require avoiding circular dependency on AWS. GCP provides comparable managed services, and Cloudflare sits in front to keep the platform cloud-portable. This also demonstrates to customers that the platform is infrastructure-agnostic.

key_decisions.title: AWS Authentication via STS AssumeRole, Never Stored Keys
key_decisions.choice: Cross-account IAM roles with AWS STS AssumeRole for all AWS operations
key_decisions.rationale: Users create an IAM role in their AWS account with a trust policy pointing to the platform's AWS identity. The platform assumes that role on demand, gets short-lived credentials, and never stores long-lived access keys — satisfying the hard security constraint and following AWS least-privilege best practices.

key_decisions.title: Deployment Orchestration via BullMQ Job Queue
key_decisions.choice: BullMQ on Redis for all deployment job scheduling and worker management
key_decisions.rationale: Deployment pipelines are inherently async, long-running, and failure-prone. BullMQ provides retry logic, priority queues, job progress tracking, delayed jobs, and dead-letter queues — handling partial failures and AWS API timeouts gracefully without blocking the API layer.

key_decisions.title: Real-Time Logs via Server-Sent Events + Redis Pub/Sub
key_decisions.choice: SSE for client delivery, Redis Pub/Sub as the internal broadcast bus
key_decisions.rationale: SSE is unidirectional (server → client), simpler than WebSockets for log streaming, and works across HTTP/2. Workers publish CloudWatch log chunks to Redis channels; the log streamer service fans them out to subscribed clients. This decouples workers from the frontend connection layer and scales horizontally.

key_decisions.title: Secrets Management via HashiCorp Vault
key_decisions.choice: HashiCorp Vault with dynamic secrets and envelope encryption
key_decisions.rationale: Environment variables and AWS credentials must be encrypted at rest and in transit. Vault provides dynamic secret leases, audit logging, fine-grained policies, and integrates with AWS Secrets Manager for per-environment injection at deploy time — meeting compliance and audit trail requirements for Engineering Managers.

key_decisions.title: IaC Generation with Claude AI
key_decisions.choice: Claude Sonnet for CloudFormation and Terraform template generation
key_decisions.rationale: Generating valid, optimized IaC from project metadata is a complex reasoning task. Claude can analyze project type, detected dependencies, target AWS service, and environment context to produce correct templates, reducing the engineering burden of maintaining hundreds of static templates and enabling long-tail AWS service support.

key_decisions.title: Microservices Architecture with tRPC for Internal Comms
key_decisions.choice: Separate services for Auth, Projects, Deployment, Environments, Analytics, and Cost — communicating via tRPC
key_decisions.rationale: Each service has distinct scaling needs (e.g., Deployment Workers scale with queue depth, Analytics scales with read load) and distinct deployment cadences. tRPC provides end-to-end type safety between Node.js services without protobuf ceremony, making internal APIs as safe as external ones.

key_decisions.title: PostgreSQL + TimescaleDB for Hybrid Data Needs
key_decisions.choice: Single PostgreSQL instance with TimescaleDB extension for time-series deployment metrics
key_decisions.rationale: Relational data (users, projects, RBAC, audit logs) and time-series data (deployment frequency, success rates, MTTR) live in one database engine, reducing operational overhead. TimescaleDB's hypertables and continuous aggregates power the analytics dashboard queries without a separate Influx or ClickHouse cluster at this scale.

# Work Orders

**Total Story Points:** 204 | **Sprints:** 10 | **Stories:** 18

---

## Epic 1: Foundation & AWS Connectivity
> Establish the core platform infrastructure, authentication system, and secure AWS account integration layer that all other features depend on.

### [E1-S1] Platform Authentication & RBAC System
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** backend, security, database

**User Story**
As a developer, I want to sign in with my GitHub or GitLab account so that I can access the platform without managing separate credentials.

**Work Description**
Implement the Auth Service using Fastify + Passport.js supporting OAuth2 flows for GitHub and GitLab. Issue short-lived JWTs (15min access, 7-day refresh) stored in HttpOnly cookies. Build the RBAC data model in PostgreSQL with roles (Owner, Admin, Deployer, Viewer) scoped to organizations and individual projects. All auth state cached in Redis 7 with session invalidation support.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] OAuth2 login flow completes successfully for GitHub and GitLab with token exchange and user profile upsert
- [ ] JWT access tokens expire in 15 minutes; refresh tokens rotate on use and invalidate on logout
- [ ] RBAC roles (Owner, Admin, Deployer, Viewer) are enforced at the API Gateway middleware level — unauthorized requests return 403
- [ ] PostgreSQL schema includes `users`, `organizations`, `org_members`, `project_members` tables with role columns and audit timestamps
- [ ] Session data (user ID, role, org context) is stored and retrieved from Redis with TTL matching token expiry
- [ ] Auth service exposes a `/auth/verify` tRPC endpoint consumed by all downstream services for token validation

---

### [E1-S2] AWS Account Connector via STS AssumeRole
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 1 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** backend, aws, security

**User Story**
As a developer, I want to connect my AWS account to the platform using an IAM role so that deployments can act on my behalf without me sharing long-lived credentials.

**Work Description**
Build the AWS Connector Layer using AWS SDK v3 STS `AssumeRole`. The platform generates a unique external ID per organization and provides a CloudFormation one-click stack template for users to create the trust-policy IAM role in their account. On connection, the platform calls `sts:AssumeRole`, validates returned credentials, and stores only the role ARN and external ID in PostgreSQL — never access keys. Short-lived credentials are generated per-operation and cached in Redis with TTL matching their 1-hour expiry.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Platform generates a unique, non-guessable external ID per org stored in PostgreSQL `aws_connections` table
- [ ] A downloadable CloudFormation template is generated with the correct trust policy, external ID condition, and least-privilege IAM permissions for ECS, Lambda, Beanstalk, CloudFormation, and CloudWatch
- [ ] `POST /aws-connections` validates AssumeRole succeeds before persisting the connection; returns a 400 with specific error if role is unreachable or missing required permissions
- [ ] Short-lived credentials are cached in Redis with a key of `aws_creds:{connection_id}` and TTL of 50 minutes (refreshed before expiry)
- [ ] Zero long-lived AWS access keys are stored anywhere in PostgreSQL, Vault, or application logs — verified by automated secret scanning in CI
- [ ] Multi-account support: a single org can register up to 10 AWS accounts, each independently connectable and selectable per project

---

### [E1-S3] Project Creation & Auto-Detection Engine
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, ai, frontend

**User Story**
As a developer, I want the platform to automatically detect my project type when I connect my repository so that I don't have to manually configure build settings.

**Work Description**
Build the Project Service with a repository scanning engine that clones or fetches the repo tree via Octokit and analyzes file signatures (`Dockerfile`, `package.json`, `requirements.txt`, `pom.xml`, `go.mod`, `serverless.yml`, `.ebextensions/`) to classify project type. Feed detection results into Claude Haiku via the Anthropic SDK for ambiguous cases and to recommend optimal target AWS service (ECS, Lambda, Elastic Beanstalk). Store project metadata and detected configuration in PostgreSQL `projects` and `project_configs` tables.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Deterministic detection (file signature matching) correctly identifies: Node.js, Python, Java, Go, and Dockerfile-based projects with >95% accuracy on a test corpus of 50 sample repos
- [ ] Claude Haiku is invoked only for ambiguous cases (when signature detection returns a confidence score below 0.8), keeping AI latency out of the happy path
- [ ] Detected project type, recommended AWS target service, suggested runtime, and estimated build command are returned in the `POST /projects` response within 10 seconds for repos under 1,000 files
- [ ] Users can override any auto-detected value through a confirmation UI step before the project is saved
- [ ] Project records are linked to org, AWS connection, and Git repo in PostgreSQL with a `detection_metadata` JSONB column storing the raw analysis
- [ ] GitHub and GitLab webhooks are registered automatically on project creation to enable future CI/CD triggers

---

### [E1-S4] Environment Management & Secrets Storage
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 2 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** backend, security, database

**User Story**
As a DevOps engineer, I want to create isolated environments (dev, staging, production) with separate configurations and secrets so that changes in one environment never affect another.

**Work Description**
Build the Environment Service backed by PostgreSQL for environment metadata and HashiCorp Vault for all secrets and environment variables. Each environment gets a dedicated Vault path (`secret/{org_id}/{project_id}/{env_name}/`). Environment variables are written to Vault with envelope encryption; at deploy time, the Deployment Orchestrator fetches secrets using a short-lived Vault token scoped to that specific environment path. Integrate with AWS Secrets Manager to optionally sync secrets at deploy time.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] `POST /environments` creates an environment record in PostgreSQL and provisions a scoped Vault policy and path — both operations succeed atomically or neither is persisted
- [ ] Environment variables set via `PUT /environments/{id}/vars` are written to Vault with AES-256 envelope encryption; they are never stored in PostgreSQL or application logs
- [ ] Secrets are injected into the deployment job context at runtime via a short-lived Vault token (TTL: 5 minutes) that is revoked immediately after the worker retrieves the values
- [ ] Environment isolation is enforced: a Deployer-role user with access to `dev` environment receives a 403 when attempting to read or write `production` secrets
- [ ] AWS Secrets Manager sync is available as an opt-in per environment, creating/updating secrets under the path `/{org}/{project}/{env}/` in the user's AWS account
- [ ] Deleting an environment cascades to revoke all Vault policies and delete the Vault path, confirmed by a Vault audit log entry

---

## Epic 2: Deployment Pipeline Orchestration
> Build the core deployment engine that takes a project from source code to running AWS infrastructure with real-time visibility and zero manual configuration.

### [E2-S1] One-Click Deployment Pipeline Engine
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 3 | **Points:** 13
**Tags:** backend, aws, infrastructure

**User Story**
As a developer, I want to deploy my application to AWS with a single click so that I can go from code to running infrastructure in under 10 minutes without touching AWS directly.

**Work Description**
Build the Deployment Orchestrator service that accepts a `POST /deployments` request, validates project + environment + AWS connection, and enqueues a BullMQ job on Redis. Deployment Workers execute a pipeline of typed stages: `SOURCE_FETCH → BUILD → PUSH_ARTIFACT → PROVISION_INFRA → DEPLOY → HEALTH_CHECK`. Each stage reports progress via Redis Pub/Sub. Workers handle AWS SDK v3 calls to ECS (`RegisterTaskDefinition`, `UpdateService`), Lambda (`UpdateFunctionCode`, `PublishVersion`), and Elastic Beanstalk (`CreateApplicationVersion`, `UpdateEnvironment`) based on project type.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] `POST /deployments` returns a `deployment_id` and `job_id` within 200ms; all heavy work is asynchronous via BullMQ
- [ ] BullMQ job includes retry logic: AWS API calls retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff (1s, 4s, 16s) before the stage is marked failed
- [ ] Each pipeline stage transitions are persisted to PostgreSQL `deployment_stages` table with `started_at`, `completed_at`, and `status` columns for full auditability
- [ ] ECS deployment: creates or updates task definition, updates service desired count, and waits for service stability using `waitUntilServicesStable` with a 10-minute timeout
- [ ] Lambda deployment: zips source or uses existing container image, calls `UpdateFunctionCode`, publishes a new version, and updates the `$LATEST` alias
- [ ] A deployment that has been queued for more than 30 minutes without starting is automatically marked `TIMED_OUT` and the BullMQ job is moved to the dead-letter queue
- [ ] New users complete their first successful deployment in under 10 minutes on a sample Node.js project (verified in integration test)

---

### [E2-S2] Real-Time Log Streaming via SSE & Redis Pub/Sub
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 3 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, frontend, infrastructure

**User Story**
As a developer, I want to see live deployment logs streamed to my browser as my deployment runs so that I can immediately identify errors without waiting for the process to finish.

**Work Description**
Build the Log Streamer service with a Redis Pub/Sub bridge. Deployment Workers publish structured log chunks (`{deployment_id, stage, timestamp, level, message}`) to Redis channel `logs:{deployment_id}`. The Log Streamer subscribes and fans out to connected clients via Server-Sent Events on `GET /deployments/{id}/logs/stream`. On the frontend, use the native `EventSource` API to consume the SSE stream and render output in an Xterm.js terminal component. CloudWatch log forwarding posts final logs to the user's CloudWatch log group post-deployment.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Log chunks appear in the browser within 500ms of being published by the worker, measured end-to-end in a local integration test
- [ ] SSE connection sends a `retry: 3000` directive so clients automatically reconnect on network interruption without losing log continuity (logs are replayed from PostgreSQL `deployment_logs` on reconnect)
- [ ] Logs are persisted to PostgreSQL `deployment_logs` table in batches of 50 or every 2 seconds (whichever comes first) so historical logs are accessible after the SSE stream closes
- [ ] Xterm.js terminal renders ANSI color codes correctly — success messages in green, errors in red, warnings in yellow
- [ ] Log stream endpoint enforces authentication: unauthenticated or unauthorized requests receive a 401 before the SSE connection is established
- [ ] CloudWatch log group `/{platform}/{org}/{project}/{env}` is created in the user's AWS account and receives the full deployment log after job completion

---

### [E2-S3] Rollback & Deployment Version History
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 4 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, frontend, aws

**User Story**
As a DevOps engineer, I want to roll back a failed production deployment to the last known good version in under 2 minutes so that I can restore service availability immediately.

**Work Description**
Every successful deployment persists a version snapshot in PostgreSQL `deployment_versions` table including artifact reference (ECR image tag, Lambda version ARN, or S3 artifact key), full environment variable hash, and IaC template hash. The rollback endpoint `POST /deployments/{id}/rollback` enqueues a high-priority BullMQ job that skips the BUILD stage and goes directly to PROVISION_INFRA → DEPLOY using the stored artifact reference. Implement automated rollback triggers: if post-deployment health checks fail 3 consecutive times within 5 minutes, auto-rollback is initiated.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] `POST /deployments/{id}/rollback` enqueues a priority-queue BullMQ job and returns within 200ms; rollback completes within 2 minutes for ECS and Lambda targets on a healthy AWS account
- [ ] Rollback jobs use the exact artifact reference (ECR image digest, not tag; Lambda version ARN, not `$LATEST`) from the target deployment version to guarantee bit-for-bit identical deployment
- [ ] PostgreSQL `deployment_versions` records include: `artifact_ref`, `env_vars_hash`, `iac_template_hash`, `git_sha`, `deployed_by`, `deployed_at` — all non-nullable
- [ ] Deployment history UI displays a paginated list of versions with git SHA, deployer name, deployment duration, and status badge; rollback button is visible only to Admin/Owner roles
- [ ] Automated rollback fires when: 3 consecutive health check pings fail within a 5-minute window post-deployment — a Slack/email notification is sent with rollback reason
- [ ] Rolling back a rollback (re-deploying the version that was rolled back from) is supported as a normal rollback operation

---

### [E2-S4] Post-Deployment Health Checks & Alerting
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** high | **Sprint:** 4 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, aws, infrastructure

**User Story**
As a DevOps engineer, I want the platform to automatically verify my application is healthy after each deployment so that I'm alerted immediately if something goes wrong without manually watching CloudWatch.

**Work Description**
After the DEPLOY stage completes, the worker spawns a HealthCheck sub-process that polls the configured health check endpoint (HTTP GET returning 2xx) or AWS service health (ECS service stability, Lambda `GetFunctionConfiguration` `State: Active`) at 15-second intervals for up to 5 minutes. Results are published to Redis Pub/Sub and streamed to the frontend. Integrate with CloudWatch Alarms API to create/update a deployment-scoped alarm per environment. Failed health checks trigger the automated rollback flow from E2-S3.

**Acceptance Criteria**
- [ ] Health check configuration accepts: URL endpoint (for HTTP checks), timeout (default 10s), expected status code (default 200), and consecutive success threshold (default 2)
- [ ] Health check results are streamed to the deployment log SSE stream in real time with `[HEALTH CHECK]` prefix and HTTP status received
- [ ] A CloudWatch Alarm is created in the user's AWS account named `{platform}-{project}-{env}-deploy-health` with `EvaluationPeriods: 3`, `Threshold: 1` on the HTTP check metric
- [ ] If health check succeeds, deployment is marked `HEALTHY` and version is eligible as a rollback target; if it fails 3 consecutive checks, automated rollback is triggered per E2-S3 criteria
- [ ] Failure notifications are sent via configured channels (email required; Slack webhook optional) within 30 seconds of the third consecutive health check failure
- [ ] Health check history is stored in TimescaleDB `health_check_events` hypertable for inclusion in the analytics dashboard

---

## Epic 3: Developer Experience & Integrations
> Deliver the Git-native CI/CD triggers, IaC generation, cost estimation, and the React dashboard that makes the platform intuitive for developers and teams.

### [E3-S1] Git Integration & CI/CD Webhook Engine
**Type:** feature | **Priority:** medium | **Sprint:** 5 | **Points:** 8
**Tags:** backend, integrations

**User Story**
As a developer, I want deployments to trigger automatically when I merge a pull request to my main branch so that my code ships without me having to visit the platform dashboard.

**Work Description**
Build the Git Integration Service using Octokit for GitHub and the GitLab/Bitbucket REST APIs. On project creation (E1-S3), register webhooks for `push` and `pull_request` events. The Webhook Handler validates HMAC signatures, parses event payloads, and applies per-project trigger rules (e.g., "deploy to staging on push to `main`; deploy to production on push to `release/*` with approval"). Valid triggers enqueue deployment jobs via tRPC call to the Deployment Orchestrator. Delivery status and re-delivery capability are stored in PostgreSQL.

# Artist Portfolio Platform — Product Requirements Document

---

> **Project Type:** Web Application — Artist Portfolio & E-Commerce
> **Complexity:** Medium
> **Estimated Timeline:** 6 Weeks
> **Estimated Operating Cost:** $25–65/month
> **Architecture:** Next.js Monolith deployed on Vercel
> **Prepared by:** Press, Documentation Agent — Forge Platform

---

## Executive Summary

Independent artists today operate in a fragmented digital landscape — their work scattered across Instagram feeds, Etsy storefronts, Behance profiles, and email inboxes. While each of these channels serves a narrow purpose, none of them serve the artist as a whole. The result is a professional identity that feels pieced together rather than intentional, and a business that leaks revenue, opportunities, and audience trust at every seam. This platform is built to fix that.

The Artist Portfolio Platform is a purpose-built, full-stack web application that gives an artist a singular, owned digital home — one that functions simultaneously as a curated gallery, a functioning art store, a commission management system, and a personal brand hub. Built on Next.js 14 with a clean PostgreSQL backend, Stripe-powered payments, and a CDN-optimized image delivery pipeline via Imgix, the platform is engineered to present artwork beautifully and perform reliably without requiring the artist to touch a line of code after launch. Every feature decision prioritizes the artist's independence and creative authority.

The platform is not a marketplace and not a social network — it is an owned channel. The artist sets the prices, owns the customer relationships, keeps the revenue, and controls the narrative. For buyers and collaborators, it delivers the clarity and professionalism that independent artist websites historically lack: transparent pricing, straightforward commission intake, and a browsing experience that does justice to the work itself. Built in six weeks on a lean, modern stack, this is a platform designed to grow with the artist from their first online sale to their hundredth.

---

## The Problem

Artists are running professional creative businesses on infrastructure that was never designed for it. Social media platforms optimize for engagement and advertising revenue — not for selling art or converting a curious follower into a paying client. Third-party marketplaces like Etsy or Redbubble extract significant commission fees, commoditize the artist's work alongside thousands of competitors, and prevent any direct relationship with buyers. Email threads and DMs become the de facto commission management system, creating chaos, dropped inquiries, and an unprofessional client experience.

The consequence is threefold. First, the artist loses money — to platform fees, to missed inquiries, to buyers who couldn't figure out how to purchase. Second, the artist loses time — context-switching between five tools to manage what should be a unified workflow. Third, and most damaging long-term, the artist loses brand equity — their professional identity is defined by someone else's platform, subject to algorithm changes, deplatforming risk, and a visual aesthetic they don't control.

Buyers and collaborators feel this dysfunction too. Finding and purchasing art directly from an independent artist is unnecessarily difficult. Pricing is unclear, availability is guesswork, and the process for requesting a commission varies wildly — if it exists at all. The market for independent art is real and growing, but friction in the buying process is leaving significant revenue on the table for artists who deserve it.

---

## The Solution

The Artist Portfolio Platform consolidates every aspect of an artist's digital business into a single, elegantly designed web application that the artist owns outright. Rather than adapting general-purpose tools to creative work, this platform is designed from the ground up around the actual workflows of a working artist — uploading new pieces, fulfilling shop orders, reviewing commission inquiries, and publishing process content to build an audience.

What makes this solution distinct is the combination of professional presentation quality with operational simplicity. High-resolution artwork is delivered through an Imgix image CDN pipeline that automatically optimizes for device and connection speed, ensuring that the work looks stunning on every screen without sacrificing load performance. Payments are handled through Stripe Checkout with full PCI DSS compliance managed at the gateway level, so the artist can accept credit cards and process transactions without taking on financial compliance liability.

The admin experience is purpose-built rather than generic. Instead of adapting a bloated CMS or learning a third-party tool, the artist manages their entire site — gallery, shop, commissions, blog — through a lightweight custom dashboard scoped to exactly the tasks they actually perform. The platform is designed to be fully self-managed within days of launch, with no developer dependency for day-to-day operations.

---

## User Personas

### Maya Chen — The Independent Artist (Site Owner)

**Background:** A professional illustrator and painter, five years into her career, currently selling through Etsy and Instagram. She has a loyal following but feels like she's "building on rented land."

**Goals:**
- Establish a professional web presence that reflects the quality and seriousness of her work
- Sell original pieces and prints without losing 15–20% to marketplace fees
- Handle commission requests in a structured, professional way that doesn't require back-and-forth emails to gather basic information
- Understand which artworks are resonating with her audience through real data

**Pain Points:**
- No single place to send potential clients that represents her full body of work
- Commission inquiries arrive through Instagram DMs, emails, and Etsy messages with no standardization
- Etsy fees and competition from mass-produced prints undercut the value of her originals
- No visibility into who is visiting her work or where they're coming from

**How Forge Helps:**
The platform gives Maya a fully owned digital home she controls end-to-end. She uploads work once to a categorized gallery, manages her shop with direct Stripe payouts, receives structured commission requests through a dedicated intake form, and tracks visitor behavior through integrated analytics — all from a single admin dashboard she can manage in minutes per week.

---

### Daniel Reyes — The Art Buyer / Collector

**Background:** A design professional in his 30s who regularly buys art for personal collection and office spaces. He prefers buying directly from artists when possible but finds the process frustrating and inconsistent.

**Goals:**
- Browse and purchase original artworks or quality prints directly from the artist
- Understand exactly what's available, at what price, and in what format before making contact
- Complete a purchase with the same confidence and ease as any other online shopping experience

**Pain Points:**
- Independent artist websites (when they exist) often feel unfinished, slow, or hard to navigate
- Pricing and availability of original pieces is rarely clearly stated
- Checkout processes are clunky or require account creation on unfamiliar platforms
- Hard to get a holistic view of an artist's range and style in one place

**How Forge Helps:**
Daniel lands on a fast, beautifully designed gallery that presents Maya's full portfolio with clear categorization and filterable browsing. The shop displays originals, prints, and digital downloads with transparent pricing and availability. Stripe Checkout provides a familiar, trusted payment flow. No guesswork, no DM required.

---

### Sophie Laurent — The Potential Client / Collaborator

**Background:** A creative director at a boutique publishing house looking to commission original illustrations for a book project. She needs to evaluate artistic range and professional credibility before committing to a project conversation.

**Goals:**
- Quickly assess whether an artist's style is the right fit for a project
- Understand the artist's process, past work, and professional background
- Initiate a commission inquiry with the relevant project details in a structured way

**Pain Points:**
- Artist websites rarely communicate professional context — exhibition history, client work, press — that validates the artist for commercial engagements
- No clear path from "I'm interested" to "here's my brief"
- Uncertainty about budget ranges, turnaround expectations, and what information to provide upfront

**How Forge Helps:**
The About & Bio page establishes Maya's professional credentials — resume, exhibition history, press mentions, and influences — giving Sophie the context she needs to make a confident decision to reach out. The Commission Request System presents a structured intake form that captures project details, budget range, size, medium, and timeline upfront, starting the engagement professionally and eliminating the information-gathering email chain.

---

## Feature Requirements

### MVP — Phase 1

*Core features required for a complete, launchable product at the end of the 6-week timeline.*

| Feature | Description | Priority | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Portfolio Gallery** | Categorized, filterable gallery with high-resolution image uploads, zoom capability, and support for organizing by medium, style, or series. Powered by Imgix for optimized delivery. | High | L |
| **Online Shop / Art Store** | Full e-commerce flow for selling originals, prints, and digital downloads. Includes product pages, inventory management, cart, and Stripe Checkout integration. | High | L |
| **Commission Request System** | Structured intake form capturing project description, medium, size, budget range, and reference images. Automated confirmation emails via Resend. Commission status tracked in admin. | High | M |
| **About & Bio Page** | Editable page for artist story, influences, exhibition history, resume/CV, and press mentions. Managed via admin dashboard, no code required. | High | S |
| **Contact Form & Social Integration** | Contact form with email notification delivery via Resend. Social profile links (Instagram, Pinterest, Behance) integrated in header and footer. | High | S |
| **Admin Dashboard** | Purpose-built CMS panel for uploading artwork, managing shop listings and inventory, reviewing commission requests, tracking orders, and publishing blog posts. | High | L |
| **Mobile-Responsive Design** | Fully optimized layout and interactions across mobile, tablet, and desktop. All purchase and inquiry flows tested and functional on iOS and Android. | High | M |
| **Authentication (Admin)** | Secure artist login via NextAuth.js protecting all admin routes. Single-owner access model. | High | S |
| **SSL & Security Baseline** | HTTPS enforced across the entire site. PCI compliance delegated to Stripe at the gateway level. Input validation via Zod on all API routes. | High | S |
| **SEO Architecture** | Server-side rendered pages with structured metadata, Open Graph tags, canonical URLs, and semantic HTML to support discoverability of artist name and individual artworks. | High | M |

---

### Phase 2 — Post-Launch Enhancements

*Features to be prioritized in the first post-launch development cycle based on real usage data and artist feedback.*

| Feature | Description | Priority | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Blog / Process Journal** | Rich-text content section for behind-the-scenes posts, work-in-progress updates, and art tips. Supports embedded images and social sharing. Improves SEO and audience connection. | Medium | M |
| **Newsletter / Mailing List Signup** | Email capture widget integrated with Mailchimp or equivalent. Subscriber management in admin. Supports announcements for new releases and exhibitions. | Medium | S |
| **Analytics & Visitor Insights Dashboard** | Google Analytics 4 and/or Plausible integration with a curated insights view in the admin dashboard showing top artworks by views, traffic sources, and shop conversion rates. | Medium | M |
| **Print Fulfillment Integration** | Integration with a print-on-demand partner (e.g., Printful or Gelato) to automate print production and shipping for print shop orders, eliminating manual fulfillment by the artist. | Medium | L |
| **Artwork Licensing Module** | Allow buyers to license digital artwork for commercial use with defined license tiers and automated agreement delivery. Opens a new revenue stream for digital works. | Low | L |
| **Exhibition / Events Page** | Dedicated page for upcoming shows, fairs, and events with location, dates, and links. Manageable from the admin dashboard. | Low | S |
| **Wishlist / Save for Later** | Allow visitors to save artworks to a personal wishlist without purchasing, with optional email notification when a saved item is running low in inventory. | Low | M |
| **Multi-Currency Support** | Display prices and process payments in the buyer's local currency, powered by Stripe's multi-currency capabilities. Reduces friction for international buyers. | Low | M |

---

## Technical Requirements

### Performance
- **Page Load Time:** Portfolio and gallery pages must load in under 3 seconds on mobile devices on a standard 4G connection, as measured by Lighthouse and Core Web Vitals
- **Image Delivery:** All artwork served through the Imgix CDN pipeline with automatic WebP conversion, responsive sizing (`srcset`), and lazy loading. Original high-resolution files stored in AWS S3 and never served directly to end users
- **Caching:** Redis via Upstash used for session management and caching frequently accessed gallery and shop data to reduce database query load
- **Edge Delivery:** Vercel Edge Network used for global CDN distribution of all static and server-rendered assets; CloudFront CDN provides an additional distribution layer for S3-originated image assets

### Security
- **Authentication:** Admin dashboard protected by NextAuth.js with secure session management. All admin API routes validate session server-side before processing any request
- **Payment Security:** PCI DSS compliance fully delegated to Stripe. The application never handles raw card data. Stripe Checkout hosted pages used for all payment flows
- **Input Validation:** All user-submitted data (contact forms, commission requests, shop orders) validated server-side using Zod schemas before database writes
- **SSL/TLS:** HTTPS enforced site-wide via Vercel's automatic SSL provisioning. HTTP requests redirected to HTTPS at the edge
- **Environment Secrets:** All API keys (Stripe, Mailchimp, Resend, AWS) stored as encrypted environment variables in Vercel. Never committed to source control
- **Database Security:** PostgreSQL hosted on Supabase with Row Level Security (RLS) policies enforced at the database layer to protect artist-only data from unauthorized access

### Scalability
- **Database:** PostgreSQL on Supabase scales vertically and supports connection pooling via PgBouncer for handling concurrent requests during traffic spikes (e.g., a new release announcement)
- **Storage:** AWS S3 provides effectively unlimited storage for a growing library of high-resolution artwork files. Storage costs scale linearly with usage at predictable rates
- **Application Hosting:** Vercel's serverless function model scales automatically with traffic. No capacity planning required for the anticipated traffic volumes at launch or in the 12 months following
- **Image Bandwidth:** Imgix CDN absorbs image delivery load independent of the application server, ensuring high-traffic gallery browsing does not impact API or checkout performance
- **Content Volume:** The database schema and admin UI are designed to support hundreds of portfolio pieces, dozens of shop listings, and years of blog content without performance degradation

### Compatibility
- **Browser Support:** Full functionality on current versions of Chrome, Safari, Firefox, and Edge. Mobile Safari (iOS) and Chrome (Android) tested explicitly for all purchase and form flows
- **Device Support:** Responsive layouts tested at 375px (mobile), 768px (tablet), and 1280px+ (desktop) breakpoints
- **Accessibility:** WCAG 2.1 AA compliance targeted, with particular attention to image alt text, keyboard navigation, and sufficient color contrast for artwork metadata and UI elements

---

key_decisions.title: Next.js Monolith over Headless CMS + Separate Backend
key_decisions.choice: Next.js 14 full-stack (App Router) as a single deployable unit
key_decisions.rationale: A solo artist site has low-to-medium complexity. Next.js handles SSR for SEO, API routes for backend logic, and ships as one Vercel deployment — eliminating operational overhead of managing separate services while still delivering excellent performance and scalability.

key_decisions.title: Supabase for Database + Storage
key_decisions.choice: PostgreSQL via Supabase with Prisma ORM
key_decisions.rationale: Supabase provides a managed Postgres database with a built-in dashboard the developer can use for admin queries, plus Row Level Security for protecting artist-only data. Prisma gives type-safe database access and clean migration management.

key_decisions.title: Imgix over Raw S3 for Image Delivery
key_decisions.choice: Store originals in S3, serve via Imgix transformations
key_decisions.rationale: High-resolution artwork files can be 20–50MB. Imgix dynamically resizes, compresses, and converts to WebP on the fly, ensuring sub-3s mobile load times while preserving original quality for downloads and print fulfillment — directly addressing the core technical constraint.

key_decisions.title: Stripe as Primary Payment Processor
key_decisions.choice: Stripe Checkout + Stripe Connect for PCI compliance
key_decisions.rationale: Stripe handles PCI DSS compliance at the gateway level, removing liability from the application. Stripe Checkout provides a hosted, conversion-optimized payment flow with minimal integration effort. PayPal added as an optional fallback for buyer preference.

key_decisions.title: Custom Admin Dashboard vs. Third-Party CMS
key_decisions.choice: Lightweight custom admin panel built into the Next.js app
key_decisions.rationale: Tools like Contentful or Sanity add monthly costs and a learning curve. A purpose-built admin UI scoped to exactly what the artist needs (upload art, manage shop, view commissions, publish posts) is simpler to use and avoids vendor lock-in on content structure.

key_decisions.title: Vercel Deployment over AWS ECS
key_decisions.choice: Vercel for hosting and edge delivery
key_decisions.rationale: Vercel's zero-config Next.js deployment, automatic preview URLs, edge SSR, and integrated CDN are ideal for a single-developer, 6-week timeline. No DevOps knowledge required, and the free/pro tiers comfortably cover expected traffic volumes at launch.

key_decisions.title: Resend for Transactional Email
key_decisions.choice: Resend as the email delivery provider
key_decisions.rationale: Commission request confirmations, order receipts, and contact form notifications need reliable delivery. Resend offers a developer-friendly API with React Email templates, generous free tier, and excellent deliverability — replacing the need to configure raw SMTP.
